# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Integrated Pest Management (IPM). List any four components of IPM.**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** IPM is a sustainable, science-based pest control strategy that combines biological, cultural, physical, and chemical methods to minimize economic, health, and environmental risks.

**Four Components:**
1. **Cultural Control** – Preventive practices like sanitation, waste removal, stock rotation (FIFO)
2. **Physical/Mechanical Control** – Traps, screens, air curtains, light traps
3. **Biological Control** – Use of natural predators (cats, parasitoid wasps) and microbial agents (*Bacillus thuringiensis*)
4. **Chemical Control** – Judicious use of rodenticides and insecticides as a **last resort**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list "chemical control" first. **Incorrect hierarchy.** Prevention and mechanical methods MUST come first. Marks deducted if hierarchy is wrong.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Cleaning and Sanitizing with suitable examples.**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Pattern, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Cleaning** | **Sanitizing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Physical removal of soil/dirt | Reduction of microbes to safe level |
| **Goal** | Remove visible contamination | Kill/inhibit microorganisms |
| **Agent** | Detergents, surfactants | Heat (82°C) or chemicals (chlorine 100 ppm) |
| **Microbial Reduction** | ~1-log (90%) | ~3-log (99.9%) |
| **Example** | Scrubbing floor with soap | Spraying 100 ppm chlorine on cleaned surface |
| **Order** | Done FIRST | Done AFTER cleaning |

**Example:** In a dairy plant, the milk pipeline is first cleaned with **CIP (Clean-in-Place)** using **2% NaOH at 75°C** followed by sanitization with **hot water at 85°C for 15 minutes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** Many students **reverse the order** — sanitizing before cleaning. This is incorrect because organic soil **inactivates sanitizers**. Always: **CLEAN → RINSE → SANITIZE.**

---

## 📗 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **different types of pests commonly found in food institutions** with examples and the damage they cause. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principles and components of an Integrated Pest Management (IPM) program** suitable for a food processing unit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

Food institutions attract diverse pests that compromise safety, quality, and consumer health. The major categories are:

**1. Rodents (Mammalian Pests):**
- **Species:** *Rattus rattus*, *Rattus norvegicus*, *Mus musculus*
- **Damage:** 
  - Droppings (≈ 25 g/day per rat)
  - Urine contamination (≈ 30 mL/day)
  - Gnawing on packaging, electrical wires (fire hazard)
  - Vectors of **Salmonella, Leptospira, Hantavirus**

**2. Insects:**
- **Stored-Product Pests:** Rice weevil (*Sitophilus oryzae*), Red flour beetle (*Tribolium castaneum*) → destroy grains, flour, spices
- **Cockroaches:** German cockroach (*Blattella germanica*) → allergens, asthma triggers, *Salmonella* carrier
- **Houseflies:** *Musca domestica* → carry **E. coli, Shigella, Vibrio cholerae**

**3. Birds:**
- Pigeon (*Columba livia*), Sparrow (*Passer domesticus*)
- Damage: Droppings (highly acidic, corrode structures), feathers in food, **histoplasmosis** transmission

**4. Microbial Pests:**
- Bacteria (*Salmonella, Listeria, E. coli O157:H7*)
- Fungi (*Aspergillus flavus* → **aflatoxin** in peanuts, maize)
- Viruses (Norovirus, Hepatitis A)

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**IPM Principles for Food Processing Unit:**

**Principle 1: Prevention (Exclusion)**
- Seal all entry points (**gaps ≤ 6 mm**)
- Install air curtains at loading docks
- Use insect-proof screens (**16 mesh**)

**Principle 2: Monitoring & Identification**
- Place **rodent bait stations every 6-10 m**
- Install **insect light traps (1 per 100-150 m²)**
- Maintain **pest sighting logs** (daily, weekly)

**Principle 3: Threshold-Based Action**
- Action threshold: **2 rodents/inspection, 5 insects/trap/week**
- Apply control only when threshold exceeded

**Principle 4: Multi-Method Integration (K-T-C-B Hierarchy)**
- **Cultural:** FIFO stock, daily waste removal, cleaning schedules
- **Physical:** Traps, light traps, screens
- **Biological:** *Trichogramma* wasps for moth eggs
- **Chemical:** Use rodenticides (anticoagulants) only as last resort in **tamper-resistant bait stations**

**Principle 5: Documentation & Review**
- Monthly pest audit reports
- Trend analysis
- Corrective actions documented

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often **omit threshold-based action**. Without thresholds, pesticide overuse is rampant and marks are deducted. Always mention **monitoring + threshold + action**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice):**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Define **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)**. Explain the **five stages of cleaning and sanitization** with the chemicals, temperatures, and concentrations used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of sanitation and pest control as Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) in the HACCP system**. Explain what happens if PRPs are not properly implemented. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of SSOPs:**
SSOPs are **written, documented procedures** that describe the cleaning and sanitizing processes, the chemicals used, their concentrations, contact times, and the personnel responsible for each task in a food institution.

**Five Stages of Sanitation:**

**Stage 1: Dry Cleaning**
- Remove loose debris, packaging waste, food particles
- Tools: Brushes, scrapers, HEPA-filtered vacuums
- Frequency: **Before and after every shift**

**Stage 2: Pre-Rinse with Water**
- Water temperature: **38-46°C** (warm water dissolves grease)
- Pressure: **Low (1.5-2.5 bar)** to prevent aerosolization of pathogens

**Stage 3: Cleaning (Detergent Application)**
- Type: **Alkaline detergent** (for fat/protein) or **Acidic detergent** (for mineral scale)
- Concentration: **1-3% w/v**
- Temperature: **50-60°C**
- Contact time: **5-20 minutes**

**Stage 4: Rinse with Potable Water**
- Removes detergent residue
- Water must meet **FSSAI potable water standards**

**Stage 5: Sanitization**
- **Heat Sanitization:** **82°C water for ≥ 30 seconds**
- **Chemical Sanitization:**
  - **Chlorine:** 50-200 ppm, contact ≥ 1 min
  - **QAC:** 200-400 ppm, contact ≥ 1 min
  - **Iodine:** 12.5-25 ppm, contact ≥ 1 min

**Verification:**
- ATP swab test: **< 100 RLU**
- Microbial swab: **< 100 CFU/cm² total plate count**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Role of PRPs in HACCP:**

**Pest Control and Sanitation are foundational PRPs** (Prerequisite Programs). They create the **baseline conditions** of hygiene necessary for the HACCP system to function effectively.

**HACCP Pyramid Hierarchy:**

1. **Foundation Level (PRPs):** Sanitation, pest control, personal hygiene, training, maintenance, supplier control
2. **Middle Level (oPRPs):** Allergen management, metal detection
3. **Top Level (CCPs):** Cooking temperature (≥ 75°C), pasteurization, metal detection at critical step

**Functions of PRPs in HACCP:**

- **Hazard Control:** Eliminate **biological, chemical, and physical hazards** at the source
- **CCP Protection:** Reduce the **number of CCPs** that need critical limits
- **Compliance:** Mandatory under **FSSAI Schedule 4, Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020)**, **USFDA 21 CFR Part 117**

**Consequences of Poor PRP Implementation:**

- **Increased CCP failures** (e.g., cooking temperature insufficient due to contaminated equipment)
- **Product recall** and **foodborne outbreaks** (e.g., **2018 Romaine lettuce E. coli outbreak**)
- **Loss of FSSAI license** and **legal liability**
- **Brand reputation damage** and consumer mistrust
- **HACCP plan declared INVALID** by third-party auditors

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** Students often confuse **PRPs with CCPs**. PRPs are **general preventive practices**, while CCPs are **specific critical steps with measurable limits**. Mixing them up costs 2-3 marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **IPM = K (Cultural) + T (Physical) + C (Chemical) + B (Biological)** — always mention in this hierarchy
- ✅ **Pest categories in food institutions:** Rodents, Insects (stored-product, crawling, flying), Birds, Microorganisms
- ✅ **Action threshold:** 2 rodents/inspection or 5 insects/trap/week
- ✅ **Bait station spacing:** 6-10 m along walls; **ILT coverage:** 1 per 100-150 m²
- ✅ **SSOP stages:** Dry clean → Pre-rinse (38-46°C) → Detergent (50-60°C) → Rinse → Sanitize
- ✅ **Sanitizer concentrations:** Chlorine **50-200 ppm**, QAC **200-400 ppm**, Iodine **12.5-25 ppm**
- ✅ **Hot water sanitization:** **82°C for ≥ 30 seconds**
- ✅ **Acceptable surface limits:** **< 100 CFU/cm²** total count, **< 10 CFU/cm²** coliform
- ✅ **ATP swab:** **< 100 RLU = clean**
- ✅ **Mesh size for pest-proofing:** **≤ 16 mesh**
- ✅ **FIFO:** First In, First Out — mandatory for stock rotation
- ✅ **FSSAI Schedule 4** + **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969** are the regulatory anchors
- ✅ **Dilution Formula:** **C₁V₁ = C₂V₂** — convert % to ppm (**5% = 50,000 ppm**)
- ✅ **CFU/cm² calculation:** N ÷ (Dilution × Area)
- ✅ **PRPs are foundation; HACCP is the apex** — without PRPs, HACCP fails
- ✅ **Always clean BEFORE sanitize** — organic soil inactivates sanitizers
- ✅ **Chlorine is corrosive to aluminum** — use QAC instead
- ✅ **Fumigation** uses phosphine (AlP) — highly toxic; licensed applicators only
- ✅ **Pest control log book** is **mandatory** for FSSAI audit compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** In **14-mark questions**, always structure your answer as: **(i) Definition → (ii) Classification → (iii) Mechanism/Process → (iv) Examples/Standards → (v) Regulatory reference → (vi) Conclusion**. This 6-part structure fetches full marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Packaging - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Food Packaging - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Food Packaging: Definition

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Packaging (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Food packaging is the science, art, and technology of enclosing or protecting food products for distribution, storage, sale, and use. It refers to the coordinated system of preparing food goods in suitable containers — including wrapping, bottling, bagging, or casing — to protect them from environmental, chemical, physical, and biological hazards while maintaining their quality, safety, integrity, and extending their shelf life.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEAGT633 – Food Quality & Safety), food packaging is treated as an **integral pillar of the food safety chain**, working in tandem with HACCP, Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), and ISO 22000 standards. It is not merely a marketing tool but a **functional barrier system** that interacts actively with the food product it protects.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of food packaging as a **"tailor-made suit of armour"** for your food.

Imagine a knight going into battle. The armour must:
1. **Fit perfectly** — neither too tight nor too loose (packaging must conform to the food's physical state and chemistry).
2. **Block arrows and weather** — protect against external threats (oxygen, moisture, microbes, light).
3. **Be lightweight enough to fight in** — the package shouldn't make the food difficult to handle or transport.
4. **Communicate identity** — the knight's colours and crest identify friend from foe (labelling, branding).
5. **Allow controlled breathing** — some armour has ventilation (permeability for fresh produce).

That is exactly what food packaging does — it is a **protective, communicative, and functional interface** between the food product, the consumer, and the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> As per the PEAGT633 2024 Scheme, students must clearly distinguish between **primary**, **secondary**, and **tertiary packaging**, since this forms the base for advanced topics like packaging materials, modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), and active/intelligent packaging covered later in Module 2.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning (as per KTU syllabus) |
|---|---|
| **Primary Packaging** | The layer in *direct contact* with the food (e.g., the inner pouch of milk, the bottle of juice). |
| **Secondary Packaging** | The outer wrapper that *groups primary packs* (e.g., the cardboard box holding 6 yoghurt cups). |
| **Tertiary Packaging** | Used for *bulk transport and logistics* (e.g., shrink-wrapped pallets, corrugated shipping boxes). |
| **Active Packaging** | Interacts with the food or headspace to *extend shelf life* (e.g., oxygen scavengers, moisture absorbers). |
| **Intelligent Packaging** | *Monitors and communicates* the state of the food (e.g., time-temperature indicators, freshness sensors). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concepts

## 2.1 The Five Core Functions of Food Packaging

According to the standard food science framework adopted by KTU, packaging performs **five core functions**, often remembered by the mnemonic **P-LA-SS-IC**:

1. **P — Protection (Physical)**  
   Shields food from mechanical shocks, vibration, compression, and impact during handling and transit.

2. **P — Protection (Physicochemical)**  
   Acts as a barrier against **oxygen, water vapour, light, dust, and aroma loss**, preserving nutritional and sensory qualities.

3. **C — Containment**  
   Allows food to be efficiently gathered into manageable units (a liquid like milk cannot be sold without containment).

4. **A — Aversion / Information (Communication)**  
   Conveys nutritional data, ingredients, batch/lot number, expiry date, manufacturer details, cooking instructions, and statutory warnings — directly linking to **Food Safety and Standards Act (FSSAI), 2006** labelling regulations in India.

5. **S — Shelf-life Extension**  
   Through barrier properties and controlled atmospheres, packaging extends the period during which food remains safe and acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:**  
> Questions on "functions of packaging" are recurring 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize the five functions in order — examiners expect a structured answer, not random points.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Packaging — The "Quality Triangle"

In KTU's outcome-based framework (aligned with NEP 2020), packaging decisions are governed by the interaction of three pillars:

- **Food** → The product's properties (pH, water activity $a_w$, fat content, respiration rate).
- **Package** → Material properties (permeability, mechanical strength, sealability).
- **Environment** → External conditions (temperature, humidity, oxygen concentration).

$$Q_{\text{product}} = f(\text{Food properties},\ \text{Package properties},\ \text{Environmental exposure})$$

Where $Q_{\text{product}}$ is the *quality index* of the food at any given time. A change in any one pillar alters the outcome — this is the **systems-thinking approach** KTU 2024 expects.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Key Point | Significance in KTU Curriculum |
|---|---|---|
| **Packaging as a Food Safety Tool** | A preventive control measure that complements HACCP by acting as a *secondary line of defence*. | CO2 (Understand food safety systems) |
| **Shelf Life** | The time period during which the food retains acceptable sensory, nutritional, and safety attributes under defined storage conditions. | CO1 (Apply quality concepts) |
| **Permeability** | The rate at which a substance (gas/vapour) passes through a packaging film, expressed in units like $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{mm} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{atm})$. | CO3 (Analyse packaging systems) |
| **Migration** | Transfer of packaging constituents into food; regulated by overall migration limit (OML) of **10 mg/dm²** (as per EU standards, also referenced in Indian context). | CO2, CO4 |
| **Hermetic Seal** | An airtight, leak-proof seal that prevents microbial entry and gas exchange. | CO3 |
| **Tamper-Evident Packaging** | Designed to show visible evidence if the package has been opened or interfered with. | CO2 (Food safety) |
| **FSSAI Compliance** | All food packaging in India must comply with the **Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Regulations, 2018**. | CO4 (Regulatory framework) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**  
> Food packaging engineers use this concept sheet to design packaging systems that balance *cost*, *protection*, *sustainability*, and *consumer convenience* — a multidisciplinary problem that blends material science, microbiology, chemistry, and logistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Packaging Concept

## 3.1 Exhaustive Definition Walkthrough

Let us dissect the formal KTU definition into its constituent components for analytical clarity:

**Step 1 — Identify the subject domain**
> *"Food packaging is the science, art, and technology..."*
> Here, the definition explicitly positions packaging as a **multidisciplinary applied science** — combining physics (barrier properties), chemistry (polymer science), biology (microbiological protection), and engineering (mechanical design).

**Step 2 — Identify the action**
> *"...of enclosing or protecting food products..."*
> The verb **"enclosing"** refers to physical containment, while **"protecting"** refers to barrier functionality. Both must be present — a box that contains food but lets oxygen in (e.g., open cardboard) is *incomplete packaging*.

**Step 3 — Identify the objectives**
> *"...for distribution, storage, sale, and use."*
> This is the **functional life-cycle of the package**. Each stage imposes different stresses:
> - *Distribution* → Mechanical stress (shocks, vibration).
> - *Storage* → Environmental stress (humidity, temperature).
> - *Sale* → Visual and informational demands (display, labelling).
> - *Use* → Convenience demands (easy open, resealability).

**Step 4 — Identify the protective scope**
> *"...from environmental, chemical, physical, and biological hazards..."*

Let us formally list these hazard categories:

$$\text{Total Hazards} = \left\{ H_{\text{env}},\ H_{\text{chem}},\ H_{\text{phys}},\ H_{\text{bio}} \right\}$$

Where:
- $H_{\text{env}}$ = Environmental hazards (oxygen, moisture, light, dust, temperature).
- $H_{\text{chem}}$ = Chemical hazards (oxidation, rancidity, migration of packaging components).
- $H_{\text{phys}}$ = Physical hazards (shock, vibration, puncture, compression).
- $H_{\text{bio}}$ = Biological hazards (microorganisms, insects, rodents, enzymatic spoilage).

**Step 5 — Identify the desired outcome**
> *"...maintaining their quality, safety, integrity, and extending their shelf life."*

These four outcomes are **measurable parameters** in food quality engineering:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality} &\rightarrow \text{Sensory attributes (taste, colour, texture)} \\
\text{Safety} &\rightarrow \text{Microbiological & toxicological safety} \\
\text{Integrity} &\rightarrow \text{Physical & chemical stability} \\
\text{Shelf life} &\rightarrow \text{Time-to-failure under defined storage conditions}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Logical Deduction: Why Packaging is Indispensable

**Premise 1:** Food is a *perishable biological substrate* — it reacts with its environment.

**Premise 2:** Modern supply chains span *hundreds of kilometres and weeks* between producer and consumer.

**Premise 3:** Consumers demand *safe, attractive, and convenient* products.

**Deduction:** A barrier system is required to *slow down* the deterioration kinetics. Packaging is that barrier.

Mathematically, the rate of food deterioration is often modelled as first-order kinetics:

$$
\frac{dQ}{dt} = -k \cdot Q(t)
$$

Where:
- $Q(t)$ = quality attribute at time $t$.
- $k$ = deterioration rate constant (depends on temperature, $a_w$, pH, oxygen).

Integrating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} &= -k \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
\ln\left(\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0}\right) &= -k \cdot t \\
Q(t) &= Q_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

The shelf life $t_s$ is reached when $Q(t_s) = Q_{\text{min}}$ (minimum acceptable quality):

$$
t_s = \frac{\ln(Q_0 / Q_{\text{min}})}{k}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU exams:**  
A *better package* (lower oxygen/moisture permeability) **reduces** $k$, thereby **increasing** $t_s$. This is the mathematical justification for why packaging extends shelf life — and is a high-yield concept for analytical questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: The Five Functions of Food Packaging

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *block-level functional architecture* of food packaging, mapping how each function interacts with the food product and the external environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Product Core"] --> B{"Food Packaging System"}
    B --> C["Function 1 Containment"]
    B --> D["Function 2 Physical Protection"]
    B --> E["Function 3 Physicochemical Barrier"]
    B --> F["Function 4 Communication and Labelling"]
    B --> G["Function 5 Shelf Life Extension"]
    C --> H["Outcome Safe and Market Ready Food"]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Consumer Satisfaction and Food Safety"]
    A --> J["Environmental Hazards Source"]
    A --> K["Biological Hazards Source"]
    J --> B
    K --> B
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- **Node A (Food Product Core):** Represents the food itself — its intrinsic properties like pH, water activity, and nutrient composition.
- **Node B (Food Packaging System):** The central functional block that processes all five functions.
- **Nodes C, D, E, F, G:** The five core functions working in parallel — not sequentially — emphasizing that packaging is a **multifunctional simultaneous system**.
- **Nodes J, K:** Hazard sources that the packaging system must intercept.
- **Node H, I:** The ultimate outcomes — safe, market-ready food reaching the consumer.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Packaging Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Food Packaging"] --> P1["Primary Packaging"]
    P --> P2["Secondary Packaging"]
    P --> P3["Tertiary Packaging"]
    P1 --> P1A["Direct Food Contact Example Milk Pouch"]
    P2 --> P2A["Grouping Unit Example Carton of 6 Bottles"]
    P3 --> P3A["Transport Unit Example Shrink Wrapped Pallet"]
    P --> AP["Active Packaging"]
    P --> IP["Intelligent Packaging"]
    AP --> APA["Example Oxygen Scavenger Sachet"]
    IP --> IPA["Example Time Temperature Indicator"]
```

### Diagram Interpretation

This diagram helps you visually classify packaging levels — a **frequently asked KTU short-answer topic**. Primary packaging is *what the consumer touches*; secondary is *what they see on the shelf*; tertiary is *what moves in logistics*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Type)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 2]**

**Q: Define food packaging. List any two functions of food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

Food packaging is the coordinated system of enclosing food products in suitable containers — such as wrappers, bottles, pouches, or boxes — to protect them from environmental, chemical, physical, and biological hazards, while maintaining their quality, safety, and extending shelf life.

Any two functions:
1. **Protection** — Acts as a barrier against oxygen, moisture, light, and microbial contamination.
2. **Communication** — Provides labelling information like ingredients, nutritional facts, manufacturing date, expiry, and statutory warnings.

*(Word count: ~60–70 words — the ideal length for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 2]**

**Q: Distinguish between primary and secondary packaging with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Packaging | Secondary Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The packaging layer in *direct contact* with the food. | The packaging layer that *groups* primary packs together. |
| **Purpose** | Protection and containment at the consumer level. | Branding, display, and additional physical protection. |
| **Example** | The inner foil pouch of a snack. | The cardboard box containing multiple snack pouches. |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:**  
> In Part B of the ESE (End Semester Examination), Module 2 typically offers an internal choice. The examiner chooses **either Question A or Question B**. Each carries sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. We provide both alternatives below.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Define food packaging in detail. Explain any four functions of food packaging with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Food packaging is the science, art, and technology of enclosing food products for distribution, storage, sale, and use. It involves wrapping, bottling, bagging, or casing food in suitable containers to protect it from environmental, chemical, physical, and biological hazards, thereby maintaining its quality, safety, integrity, and shelf life.

**Four Functions (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

1. **Protection from Physical Hazards:**  
   Packaging shields food from mechanical damage such as shock, vibration, compression, and puncture during transportation and handling.  
   *Example:* Egg trays with moulded pulp inserts prevent breakage during transit.

2. **Protection from Physicochemical Hazards:**  
   Acts as a barrier against oxygen, moisture, light, and aroma loss.  
   *Example:* Aluminium foil laminates in biscuit packets block moisture ingress and grease leakage.

3. **Containment:**  
   Allows liquids, powders, and granular food to be held in usable units.  
   *Example:* A tetra pak enables milk to be stored and transported without spillage.

4. **Communication:**  
   Provides mandatory labelling information — ingredients, nutritional values, FSSAI logo, batch number, manufacturing and expiry dates, cooking instructions.  
   *Example:* The printed wrapper on a chocolate bar carries all required consumer information.

*(Optional 5th function for added depth — Shelf-life extension via MAP and vacuum packaging.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the role of packaging in food safety. Briefly describe primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Packaging in Food Safety (3 marks):**

Packaging acts as the **final physical barrier** against contamination after food has been processed. It:
- Prevents microbial recontamination post-processing.
- Controls oxygen and moisture ingress, slowing spoilage.
- Prevents physical entry of foreign matter (dust, insects).
- Provides tamper-evidence to deter adulteration.
- Serves as a **preventive control** within the HACCP framework — complementing, not replacing, processing safety measures.

**Three Levels of Packaging (4 marks):**

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Direct contact layer; final consumer unit. | The plastic pouch of a ready-to-eat meal. |
| **Secondary** | Groups primary packs; provides branding & display. | A printed cardboard box holding 10 pouches. |
| **Tertiary** | Bulk handling and logistics layer. | A shrink-wrapped pallet of 50 such boxes. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> Students often confuse *secondary* and *tertiary* packaging. **Memory trick:**  
> *Secondary = Shop-level* (what you see on a retail shelf).  
> *Tertiary = Transport-level* (what a truck carries).  
> Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** in a typical 7-mark answer.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the importance of food packaging in extending the shelf life of food products. Use the first-order deterioration kinetics equation to justify your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Importance of Packaging in Shelf-Life Extension (3 marks):**
- Limits oxygen ingress → slows lipid oxidation and aerobic microbial growth.
- Controls moisture transfer → prevents both drying and moisture absorption.
- Blocks light → prevents photo-oxidation of fats and vitamins.
- Provides hermetic seal → prevents entry of spoilage organisms.

**Mathematical Justification (4 marks):**

Food quality deteriorates with time. Using first-order kinetics:

$$
Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
$$

Where:
- $Q_0$ = initial quality.
- $k$ = deterioration rate constant.
- $t$ = storage time.

For a critical quality attribute reaching its minimum acceptable level $Q_{\text{min}}$, the shelf life is:

$$
t_s = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_{\text{min}}}\right)
$$

**How packaging affects $k$:**  
A packaging material with **low oxygen permeability** (e.g., EVOH-laminated films) reduces the effective $k$ for oxidation-driven deterioration. Since $k$ appears in the denominator, even a modest reduction in $k$ causes a *proportional increase* in $t_s$.

**Example:**  
A fruit juice in glass has $k = 0.05/\text{day}$ at 25°C.  
If packaged in aseptic tetra pak, $k$ drops to $0.01/\text{day}$.  
Thus $t_s$ increases fivefold — matching commercial claims of 6-month shelf life.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: What are active and intelligent packaging? Give two examples of each and explain their relevance in modern food safety.**

**Model Solution:**

**Active Packaging (3.5 marks):**
Active packaging interacts with the food or its headspace to **prolong shelf life** by modifying the internal environment.

*Examples:*
1. **Oxygen Scavenger Sachets** — Iron-based sachets placed inside dried food packets absorb residual oxygen, preventing oxidation.
2. **Moisture Absorbers** — Silica gel pads inside dried meat or spice packets control water activity ($a_w$).

**Intelligent Packaging (3.5 marks):**
Intelligent packaging **monitors and communicates** the condition of the food product during storage and transport, without altering its environment.

*Examples:*
1. **Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI)** — Colour-changing labels that visually indicate cumulative thermal exposure, used in cold-chain dairy products.
2. **Freshness Sensors** — pH-based colour indicators on fish packaging that change colour as amine compounds increase during spoilage.

**Relevance to Modern Food Safety:**
- Aligns with **FSSAI's preventive approach** to food safety.
- Enables **real-time quality monitoring** — a key Industry 4.0 trend.
- Reduces food wastage by providing accurate shelf-life information.
- Complements **ISO 22000** and **HACCP** systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> Students frequently **mix up** active and intelligent packaging.  
> **Active = ACTS** (it changes the environment inside the pack).  
> **Intelligent = INFORMS** (it tells you what is happening).  
> A clear answer that *contrasts* the two earns full marks; a confused answer loses **3–4 marks**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 | Food Packaging Definition**

- ✅ Food packaging is a **multidisciplinary applied science**, not just "wrapping food."
- ✅ The five core functions can be remembered via **Containment, Protection, Communication, Shelf-life, Convenience**.
- ✅ **Primary = direct contact**; **Secondary = shelf-level grouping**; **Tertiary = transport logistics**.
- ✅ Packaging is a **preventive control measure** in the HACCP framework.
- ✅ The **deterioration rate constant $k$** is what packaging reduces — directly extending shelf life $t_s$.
- ✅ **Active packaging acts** (modifies environment); **intelligent packaging informs** (monitors status).
- ✅ Always mention **FSSAI Packaging Regulations, 2018** in answers touching Indian regulatory context.
- ✅ Use the **five-hazard framework**: environmental, chemical, physical, biological, plus regulatory compliance.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, **structure sub-parts clearly** with sub-headings and tables — examiners reward presentation.
- ✅ Remember: **Permeability units** are $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{mm} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{atm})$; **OML** = $10 \text{ mg/dm}^2$.
- ✅ Always give **at least one real-world example** per function — abstract answers lose marks.
- ✅ The **first-order kinetics equation** $Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ is the most powerful mathematical tool for justifying shelf-life arguments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Science and Quality Assurance**, the term **"Functions of Food"** refers to the comprehensive set of roles, purposes, and physiological/psychological contributions that food performs beyond mere satiety. According to standard food science lexicons adopted by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum, food functions are systematically classified into **five primary domains**: *Nutritional, Sensory, Physiological/Health, Psychological, and Social*.

These functions form the foundational pillars upon which **food quality**, **consumer acceptability**, and **safety standards** are evaluated. A food product that fails in any one of these functional categories cannot be deemed of "high quality" regardless of its safety status.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of food functions is a high-weightage topic in Module 2 because it directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2** — *Apply quality attributes and functional properties of food in evaluating food products.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of food as a **multi-purpose Swiss Army Knife** rather than just a "fuel-tank filler":

- Just as the knife has a **blade** (cuts), **scissors** (cuts paper), **screwdriver** (repairs), and **corkscrew** (opens bottles) — each serving a different *function* — food simultaneously **nourishes the body** (nutritional), **delights the senses** (sensory), **protects against disease** (physiological), **comforts the mind** (psychological), and **binds communities** (social).

- A KTU student must understand that a food technologist is not just a "cook" but a **systems engineer** who optimizes all five functions simultaneously.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Parameters

The primary standard metrics used in evaluating food functions are:

- **Energy Density:** $4.0$ kcal/g for carbohydrates, **$4.0$ kcal/g** for proteins, **$9.0$ kcal/g** for fats
- **Reference Daily Intake (RDI):** Standardized by **ICMR-NIN (2020)** guidelines
- **Body Mass Index (BMI):** $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{\text{Weight (kg)}}{[\text{Height (m)}]^2}$
- **Glycemic Index (GI):** Scale of **$0$ to $100$**
- **Antioxidant Activity:** Measured in **$\mu$mol TE/g** (Trolox Equivalents per gram)
- **Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA):** Expressed in **g/day** or **mg/day**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always cite the **ICMR 2020 RDA values** in numerical problems; the 2010 values are outdated and may attract partial deduction in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Classification of Food Functions

The five principal functions of food can be analyzed as follows:

#### **A. Nutritional Function**
- **Definition:** Provision of macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals, water) required for growth, maintenance, and repair of body tissues.
- **Why it matters:** Nutritional inadequacy leads to **Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM)**, deficiency diseases (e.g., Scurvy, Beriberi, Rickets).
- **How it is quantified:** Through **proximate analysis** (Moisture, Ash, Fat, Protein, Carbohydrate).

#### **B. Sensory Function**
- **Definition:** Stimulation of the five human senses — *appearance, color, texture, flavor (taste + smell), and sound* — to provide eating pleasure.
- **Why it matters:** Even nutritionally perfect food will be rejected if sensory attributes are poor. Sensory quality drives **$60$–$70\%$** of consumer purchase decisions.
- **How it is measured:** Through **9-point Hedonic Scale** sensory evaluation panels (ISO 8587:2006).

#### **C. Physiological / Health Function**
- **Definition:** Provision of bioactive compounds (dietary fiber, antioxidants, probiotics, omega-3 fatty acids) that regulate body functions and reduce disease risk.
- **Why it matters:** This is the foundation of **Functional Foods** and **Nutraceuticals** — a multi-billion dollar industry.
- **How it is verified:** Through **in-vitro assays** (DPPH, ABTS) and **clinical trials**.

#### **D. Psychological Function**
- **Definition:** Food's role in mood regulation, stress relief, emotional comfort, and cognitive satisfaction ("comfort foods").
- **Why it matters:** Connects food to **mental well-being**; increasingly important in modern psychosomatic nutrition.

#### **E. Social Function**
- **Definition:** Role of food in cultural identity, religious practices, social bonding, hospitality, and status expression.
- **Why it matters:** Determines market acceptance across **diverse demographic segments**; critical for product globalization.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy from Macronutrients** | $E = (4 \times P) + (4 \times C) + (9 \times F)$ | kcal/100g | Proximate analysis energy calculation |
| **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $\text{PER} = \dfrac{\text{Weight Gain (g)}}{\text{Protein Consumed (g)}}$ | dimensionless | Nutritional quality of proteins |
| **Biological Value (BV)** | $\text{BV} = \dfrac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100$ | % | Protein quality assessment |
| **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)** | $\text{NPU} = \text{BV} \times D \times 100$ | % | Practical protein utilization |
| **Net Dietary Protein Energy % (NDpE%)** | $\text{NDpE\%} = \dfrac{P \times 4}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Protein adequacy in diet |
| **Specific Dynamic Action (SDA)** | Energy cost of digestion $\approx 5$–$30\%$ of intake | kcal | Metabolic load of food |
| **Satiety Index** | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{\text{Food Score}}{\text{Reference (White bread = 100)}}$ | dimensionless | Consumer fullness perception |
| **Sensory Score (Hedonic)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | dimensionless | Mean panel score calculation |
| **Antioxidant Capacity** | $\text{TEAC} = \dfrac{\Delta A_{\text{sample}}}{\Delta A_{\text{Trolox}}} \times C$ | $\mu$mol TE/g | DPPH/ABTS assays |
| **Glycemic Load (GL)** | $\text{GL} = \dfrac{\text{GI} \times \text{CHO (g)}}{100}$ | dimensionless | Diet planning for diabetics |

Where:
- $P$ = Protein (g), $C$ = Carbohydrate (g), $F$ = Fat (g) per 100g
- $D$ = Digestibility coefficient
- $x_i$ = Individual sensory score, $n$ = Number of panelists

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The PER and BV formulas are routinely applied in the **formulation of weaning foods, sports nutrition products, and clinical diets** — direct industry relevance for B.Tech Food Technology graduates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivation — Energy Calculation of a Food Product

**Problem Statement:** A formulated food product contains the following proximate composition per $100$ g:
- Protein $= 12$ g
- Carbohydrate $= 60$ g
- Fat $= 8$ g
- Moisture $= 15$ g
- Ash $= 5$ g

**Step 1: Verify Proximate Composition Totals (should sum to $100$ g)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Solids} &= P + C + F + \text{Ash} \\
&= 12 + 60 + 8 + 5 \\
&= 85 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The remaining $100 - 85 = 15$ g is moisture, which matches the given value. Hence, the proximate data is internally consistent.

**Step 2: Apply the Energy Equation using Atwater Factors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= (4 \times P) + (4 \times C) + (9 \times F) \\
&= (4 \times 12) + (4 \times 60) + (9 \times 8) \\
&= 48 + 240 + 72 \\
&= 360 \text{ kcal per 100 g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each macronutrient contributes energy based on its specific Atwater factor — carbohydrates and proteins at $4$ kcal/g, fats at $9$ kcal/g.

**Step 3: Calculate Percentage Energy Contribution of Each Macronutrient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{\text{Protein}} &= \frac{48}{360} \times 100 = 13.33\% \\
\%E_{\text{Carbohydrate}} &= \frac{240}{360} \times 100 = 66.67\% \\
\%E_{\text{Fat}} &= \frac{72}{360} \times 100 = 20.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Net Dietary Protein Energy Percentage (NDpE%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NDpE\%} &= \frac{P \times 4}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12 \times 4}{360} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{48}{360} \times 100 \\
&= 13.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The NDpE% indicates that protein contributes $13.33\%$ of total dietary energy — a value comfortably within the **ICMR recommended range of 10–15%** for balanced diets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-5 Engineering Interpretation:** The product is a **carbohydrate-dominant** energy source with adequate protein. Such a profile is typical of **breakfast cereals, energy bars, and glucose-based recovery drinks** used in sports nutrition.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Food Function vs. Quality Dimension

The following matrix maps each food function to its corresponding **quality evaluation parameter**:

| **Food Function** | **Primary Quality Parameter** | **Standard Test Method** | **Acceptable Range / Benchmark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nutritional | Macronutrient ratio | AOAC Proximate Analysis | P: 10–15%, C: 55–70%, F: 15–30% |
| Nutritional | Micronutrient density | HPLC, ICP-MS | Meets ICMR RDA values |
| Sensory | Color | Hunter Lab Colorimeter | L*, a*, b* within product spec |
| Sensory | Texture | Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) | Hardness, Cohesiveness per product |
| Sensory | Flavor | Gas Chromatography (GC-MS) | Aroma profile within consumer acceptance |
| Physiological | Antioxidant activity | DPPH, FRAP assay | $> 50$% inhibition at standard conc. |
| Physiological | Dietary fiber | Enzymatic-Gravimetric | $\geq 3$ g/serving for "high fiber" claim |
| Psychological | Mood-linked sensory score | Consumer panel + POMS scale | Positive correlation with comfort index |
| Social | Cultural acceptability | Ethnographic survey | $\geq 70\%$ acceptance in target population |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Five Domains of Food Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> B1[Macronutrients - P C F]

    C --> C1[Visual - Color Shape]

    C --> C3[Tactile - Texture Mouthfeel]
    C --> C4[Auditory - Crunch Sound]

    D --> D1[Bioactive Compounds]
    D --> D2[Dietary Fiber]

    E --> E1[Comfort Foods]

    F --> F1[Cultural Identity]

```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Food Function Optimization in Product Development

```mermaid

    P4 --> P6[Bioactivity Testing]:::step
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Verification Notes

- All node IDs use **purely alphanumeric identifiers** (`A`, `B1`, `P1`) — no reserved keywords used as standalone nodes.
- All node labels contain **plain uppercase text** without markdown formatting (no `**bold**`, `*italic*`, or HTML inside quoted labels).
- Color theming uses **standard CSS color names** for maximum renderer compatibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Functions of Food" and list any four primary functions with one-line explanations.*

**Model Answer:**
Functions of food refer to the diverse roles that food plays in human life beyond mere hunger satisfaction. The four primary functions are:
1. **Nutritional Function** — Supplying macronutrients and micronutrients for body growth, maintenance, and energy.
2. **Sensory Function** — Providing pleasure through appearance, aroma, taste, and texture.
3. **Physiological Function** — Delivering bioactive compounds (fiber, antioxidants, probiotics) that promote health and prevent disease.
4. **Social Function** — Serving as a medium of cultural expression, religious observance, and social bonding.

*(Word limit: As per KTU norms, answer should be concise — approx. 80–100 words.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between "Nutritional Quality" and "Sensory Quality" of food. Give two examples of quality tests for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Nutritional Quality** | **Sensory Quality** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Adequacy of nutrients for body needs | Pleasantness of perception through senses |
| Focus | Health and growth | Eating pleasure and acceptance |
| Test Example 1 | Proximate analysis (AOAC) | 9-Point Hedonic Scale test |
| Test Example 2 | Vitamin estimation by HPLC | Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the various functions of food in detail. Discuss the **Nutritional and Physiological functions** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A food product contains $15$ g protein, $65$ g carbohydrate, and $5$ g fat per $100$ g. Calculate the total energy in kcal and determine the percentage energy contribution from each macronutrient. Comment on whether the product is balanced as per ICMR recommendations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The functions of food are classified into **five major categories**:

1. **Nutritional Function** — Food provides nutrients (proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins, minerals, water) essential for body building, energy supply, and regulation of metabolic processes. *Example: Milk provides calcium for bone formation; rice provides carbohydrates for energy.*

2. **Physiological/Health Function** — Food supplies bioactive substances that prevent disease and promote wellness. *Example: Oats contain $\beta$-glucan (soluble fiber) that lowers serum cholesterol; Yogurt contains *Lactobacillus* probiotics that improve gut health; Turmeric contains curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.*

3. **Sensory Function** — Food stimulates the five senses — sight, smell, taste, touch (texture), and hearing (sound of crunch). *Example: Crisp apple provides visual appeal, aroma, sweet-sour taste, and audible crunch.*

4. **Psychological Function** — Food provides emotional satisfaction, comfort, and stress relief. *Example: Warm soup during illness; chocolate as a mood elevator.*

5. **Social Function** — Food acts as a cultural symbol, social binder, and hospitality token. *Example: Biryani in Indian celebrations; Thanksgiving turkey in Western culture.*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Apply Atwater Energy Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= (4 \times P) + (4 \times C) + (9 \times F) \\
&= (4 \times 15) + (4 \times 65) + (9 \times 5) \\
&= 60 + 260 + 45 \\
&= 365 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Percentage Energy Contributions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{\text{Protein}} &= \frac{60}{365} \times 100 = 16.44\% \\
\%E_{\text{Carbohydrate}} &= \frac{260}{365} \times 100 = 71.23\% \\
\%E_{\text{Fat}} &= \frac{45}{365} \times 100 = 12.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with ICMR Recommendations**

| **Macronutrient** | **Calculated %** | **ICMR Recommended %** | **Status** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protein | $16.44\%$ | $10$–$15\%$ | Slightly high |
| Carbohydrate | $71.23\%$ | $55$–$70\%$ | Slightly high |
| Fat | $12.33\%$ | $15$–$30\%$ | Slightly low |

**Conclusion:** The product is **moderately balanced** but has marginal deviations. For optimum balance, the fat content should be increased to $20$–$25\%$ and carbohydrates reduced to $60$–$65\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Functional Foods and Nutraceuticals**. Discuss their role in disease prevention with three examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the **9-Point Hedonic Scale**? How is it used to evaluate the sensory quality of a newly developed food product? Explain the calculation of mean sensory score with a numerical example of $5$ panelists rating a product on $8$ sensory attributes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Functional Foods** are foods that provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition due to the presence of bioactive components. **Nutraceuticals** are isolated or purified bioactive compounds delivered in pharmaceutical form (capsules, tablets) but derived from food sources.

**Role in Disease Prevention — Three Examples:**

1. **Omega-3 Fatty Acids (from Fish Oil):** Reduce triglycerides, prevent cardiovascular diseases, and reduce inflammation. *Recommended intake: 250–500 mg/day (EPA + DHA).*

2. **Probiotics (from Yogurt, Kefir):** Live microorganisms that restore gut microflora, improve digestion, enhance immunity, and prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea. *Example strain: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG.*

3. **Plant Sterols/Stanols (from Fortified Margarines):** Inhibit cholesterol absorption in the intestine, reducing LDL cholesterol by 7–12.5% with daily intake of 1.5–3 g.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**9-Point Hedonic Scale** is a standardized sensory evaluation tool (ISO 8587:2006) where panelists rate their liking on a scale from $1$ to $9$:
- $9$ = Like Extremely
- $5$ = Neither Like nor Dislike
- $1$ = Dislike Extremely

**Procedure for Newly Developed Product:**
1. Recruit a trained panel of $20$–$30$ members.
2. Serve samples in randomized, coded form under controlled conditions.
3. Panelists rate each sensory attribute independently.
4. Record scores in a structured scorecard.

**Numerical Example — Mean Sensory Score Calculation:**

Suppose $5$ panelists (P1 to P5) rate a new protein bar on the following attributes (scale 1–9):

| **Attribute** | **P1** | **P2** | **P3** | **P4** | **P5** | **Sum** | **Mean** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Color | 7 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 39 | 7.8 |
| Flavor | 8 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 39 | 7.8 |
| Texture | 6 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 7 | 34 | 6.8 |
| Aroma | 7 | 8 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 39 | 7.8 |
| Sweetness | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 | 8 | 40 | 8.0 |
| Aftertaste | 5 | 6 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 31 | 6.2 |
| Mouthfeel | 7 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 39 | 7.8 |
| Overall Acceptability | 7 | 8 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 40 | 8.0 |

**Step 1: Grand Mean Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_{\text{grand}} &= \frac{(7.8 + 7.8 + 6.8 + 7.8 + 8.0 + 6.2 + 7.8 + 8.0)}{8} \\
&= \frac{60.2}{8} \\
&= 7.525
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**
A mean score of $7.525$ (between "Like Moderately" and "Like Very Much") indicates **good consumer acceptance**. The product is commercially viable, with **aftertaste** being the weakest attribute requiring reformulation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — Always write kcal, g, %, etc. *(−0.5 marks per instance)*
> 2. **Using outdated RDA values** — Refer only to **ICMR 2020** guidelines, not 2010 versions.
> 3. **Confusing "Sensory Quality" with "Sensory Function"** — Quality is a *measurable characteristic*; function is a *purpose/role*.
> 4. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — Always include at least one **flowchart/classification chart** for full marks in classification-type questions.
> 5. **Generic answers in Part A** — Be specific: cite at least one *example* and one *standard test method* for each function.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify proximate data consistency** — Always confirm protein + carbohydrate + fat + ash + moisture = 100 g before calculations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Five Functions of Food:** Nutritional, Sensory, Physiological, Psychological, Social.
- [ ] **Atwater Factors (Memorize):** Protein = $4$ kcal/g, Carbohydrate = $4$ kcal/g, Fat = $9$ kcal/g.
- [ ] **Proximate Analysis Parameters:** Moisture, Ash, Fat, Protein, Carbohydrate (by difference).
- [ ] **ICMR 2020 Energy Distribution:** Protein 10–15%, Carbohydrate 55–70%, Fat 15–30%.
- [ ] **Sensory Evaluation Standard:** 9-Point Hedonic Scale (ISO 8587:2006).
- [ ] **Key Protein Quality Indices:** PER, BV, NPU, NDpE%.
- [ ] **Functional Foods Examples:** Oats ($\beta$-glucan), Yogurt (probiotics), Fish oil (omega-3), Turmeric (curcumin).
- [ ] **Sensory Attributes (5):** Color, Flavor, Texture, Aroma, Sound (audible crunch).
- [ ] **BMI Formula:** $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{W \text{ (kg)}}{H^2 \text{ (m}^2\text{)}}$.
- [ ] **Antioxidant Assay Standard:** DPPH, FRAP, ABTS — reported in $\mu$mol TE/g.
- [ ] **Glycemic Load Equation:** $\text{GL} = \dfrac{\text{GI} \times \text{CHO (g)}}{100}$.
- [ ] **Always cite sources:** ISO standards, AOAC methods, ICMR-NIN guidelines in exam answers.
- [ ] **Dietary Fiber Claim Threshold:** $\geq 3$ g/serving for "High Fiber" claim (FSSAI, 2020).
- [ ] **Probiotic Strain Standard:** Minimum $10^6$ CFU/g at the time of consumption (ICMR).
- [ ] **Social Function Elements:** Culture, Religion, Hospitality, Status — remember all four.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Food Quality & Safety, **Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of foods, food hazards, food quality parameters, and food components into defined groups based on established scientific, regulatory, and quality criteria. Classification provides a structured framework that enables food technologists, quality assurance professionals, and regulatory bodies to evaluate, control, and communicate about food products using a common technical language.

The Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), and international bodies such as the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **United States Food and Drug Administration (USFDA)** have all defined standardized classification systems that govern global food trade, safety assessment, and labeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Classification in Food Quality & Safety is the process of grouping foods, additives, contaminants, and quality attributes into scientifically defined categories that serve as the basis for setting specifications, applying regulations, designing safety protocols, and ensuring consistent product grading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking into a massive library containing every type of food in the world — millions of items. Without any organization, finding anything or comparing two products would be impossible. **Classification is the library's cataloging system.**

Just as a librarian sorts books by genre, author, and difficulty level, food scientists sort foods by:
- **Shelf life** (how quickly they spoil) — like sorting books by reading time
- **Acidity** (pH) — like sorting by thickness
- **Water content** — like sorting by dryness
- **Origin** (animal vs. plant) — like sorting by fiction vs. non-fiction
- **Risk level** (high-risk vs. low-risk for pathogens) — like marking books "Handle with Care"

This cataloging is what allows a food inspector in Kerala to apply the same standards used in Tokyo or Toronto, ensuring food quality and safety are universal and measurable.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The foundational metrics used universally for food classification include:

- **pH**: A measure of acidity/alkalinity, ranging from **0 (highly acidic)** to **14 (highly alkaline)**, with **7 being neutral**.
- **Water Activity (a<sub>w</sub>)**: The ratio of partial vapor pressure of water in a food to that of pure water, ranging from **0.0 (bone dry)** to **1.0 (pure water)**.
- **Perishability Index**: Qualitative measure of how quickly a food deteriorates under normal storage.
- **Microbial Load Tolerance**: Measured in **Colony Forming Units per gram (CFU/g)**.
- **Standard Plate Count (SPC)**: Threshold limits for acceptable bacterial populations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations:** Questions on classification test both *recall* (Bloom's Level 1) and *application* (Bloom's Level 3). Students are expected to know not just the categories, but also the **basis (criterion) of classification**, **examples** falling in each category, and the **practical safety implications**.

### 1.4 The Five Primary Avenues of Food Classification

In Module 2, "Classification" encompasses five interconnected dimensions:

1. **Classification of Foods** (based on origin, composition, perishability, pH, water activity)
2. **Classification of Food Quality Attributes** (sensory, nutritional, hidden attributes)
3. **Classification of Food Hazards** (biological, chemical, physical)
4. **Classification of Food Additives** (functional classes)
5. **Classification of Food Standards and Grades** (AGMARK, FSSAI, Codex grades)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Foods Based on Perishability

This is the **most frequently asked classification** in KTU examinations under Module 2.

**Operational Logic:**
- Foods are grouped by their **shelf life** at ambient storage conditions (typically 20–25°C).
- This classification is the **foundation for cold chain logistics** and **HACCP plan design**.

**Categories:**

| Category | Shelf Life | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perishable** | 1–7 days | High moisture + active enzymes + neutral pH = rapid microbial growth | Fresh milk, meat, fish, leafy vegetables, ripe fruits |
| **Semi-perishable** | Weeks to months | Reduced water activity or natural barriers | Potatoes, onions, apples, cheese, eggs |
| **Non-perishable (Stable)** | Months to years | Low water activity, low pH, or sterilized | Rice, wheat, sugar, salt, dried pulses, honey |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Perishability is *not* a fixed property of the food alone — it is a function of **storage temperature, humidity, packaging, and initial microbial load**. Pasteurized milk is perishable, while UHT milk is semi-stable.

### 2.2 Classification Based on pH (Acidity)

This classification is **critical for thermal processing and pathogen control** — a high-yield KTU topic.

**Operational Logic:**
- The pH of a food directly determines which **pathogens can grow** and the **intensity of heat treatment** required.

**Categories (per FDA / Codex standards):**

| pH Range | Category | Microbial Concern | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **< 3.7** | High Acid | Spoilage yeasts/molds; *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow | Lemons, vinegar, pickles |
| **3.7 – 4.5** | Acid | Pathogens inhibited; spoilage bacteria still active | Tomatoes, oranges, pineapple |
| **4.5 – 4.6** | Medium Acid | Boundary zone — *C. botulinum* may survive | Some fruits, fermented products |
| **> 4.6** | Low Acid | *Clostridium botulinum* and other pathogens thrive | Milk, meat, fish, vegetables, soups |

> [!NOTE]
> **The pH 4.6 Rule:** Below pH 4.6, *Clostridium botulinum* (the most dangerous food pathogen) cannot grow and produce toxin. This is the **regulatory dividing line** between high-acid and low-acid foods and dictates whether a product needs **botulinum cook** (12D reduction) or just pasteurization.

### 2.3 Classification Based on Water Activity (a<sub>w</sub>)

This is the **single most important intrinsic factor** governing microbial growth in food.

**Operational Logic:**
- Water activity (a<sub>w</sub>) measures the **availability of water** for microbial and chemical reactions, not just total water content.
- A dried food can have 20% moisture but a<sub>w</sub> = 0.3 (microbiologically stable).
- A fresh fruit can have 90% moisture but a<sub>w</sub> = 0.99 (highly perishable).

**Categories (per Scott's classification, 1957):**

| a<sub>w</sub> Range | Category | Microbial Growth | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.00 – 0.95** | High Moisture | Most bacteria, yeasts, molds | Fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, milk |
| **0.94 – 0.91** | Intermediate Moisture | Most bacteria inhibited; some yeasts/molds grow | Dried fruits, cakes, cheese |
| **0.90 – 0.80** | Semi-moist | Only xerophilic molds, osmophilic yeasts | Jam, honey, syrups |
| **< 0.60** | Low Moisture | No microbial growth | Crackers, dried spices, milk powder |

### 2.4 Classification of Food Hazards

A high-frequency KTU question category — frequently tested as "Discuss the classification of food hazards with examples."

**Three Primary Categories (per WHO / Codex):**

**A. Biological Hazards**
- Pathogenic bacteria (*Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria*, *C. botulinum*)
- Viruses (Hepatitis A, Norovirus)
- Parasites (*Taenia*, *Giardia*)
- Prions (BSE agent)
- Toxigenic fungi (Aspergillus flavus → aflatoxin)

**B. Chemical Hazards**
- **Natural toxins** (cyanogenic glycosides in cassava, solanine in potatoes)
- **Pesticide residues**
- **Heavy metals** (lead, mercury, cadmium, arsenic)
- **Veterinary drug residues** (antibiotics, hormones)
- **Food additives** in excess
- **Allergens** (the "Big 8": milk, eggs, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy)

**C. Physical Hazards**
- Glass fragments
- Metal shavings
- Bone fragments
- Plastic pieces
- Stones, hair, insect parts

### 2.5 Classification of Food Additives (Functional Classes)

As per **Codex Alimentarius** and **FSSAI regulations**, food additives are classified by their **function**, not their chemistry.

| Functional Class | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Preservatives** | Inhibit microbial growth | Sodium benzoate, BHA, BHT |
| **Antioxidants** | Prevent lipid oxidation | Ascorbic acid, tocopherols |
| **Emulsifiers** | Stabilize oil-water mixtures | Lecithin, mono/diglycerides |
| **Thickeners / Stabilizers** | Modify viscosity | Xanthan gum, pectin, carrageenan |
| **Colorants** | Restore or add color | Tartrazine, caramel, annatto |
| **Flavor Enhancers** | Boost existing flavors | MSG (Monosodium glutamate) |
| **Sweeteners** | Provide sweetness | Sucrose, aspartame, stevia |
| **Leavening Agents** | Produce CO<sub>2</sub> for rising | Baking soda, baking powder |
| **Anticaking Agents** | Prevent clumping | Silicon dioxide, calcium silicate |

### 2.6 Classification of Food Quality Attributes

Per **Kramer and Twigg (1962)** and **ITC Geneva Guidelines**, food quality has three classical dimensions:

1. **Quality of Identity** (Sensory attributes — appearance, texture, flavor, aroma)
2. **Quality of Acceptability** (Compositional/nutritional standards)
3. **Quality of Keeping** (Shelf life and stability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In exam answers, always link classification to **practical application**. A good answer does not just list categories — it explains *why* the classification was created and *how* it is used in industry (e.g., HACCP design, regulatory compliance, consumer labeling).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Definition / Use | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | Acidity / alkalinity | dimensionless | Food categorization, pathogen control |
| 2 | $a_{w} = \dfrac{P_{food}}{P_{pure\ water}}$ | Water availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Shelf life prediction, microbial limits |
| 3 | $T_{cook} = f(pH)$ | Heat intensity required | °C, minutes | Thermal process design |
| 4 | $Log_{10}\ reduction = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_{0}}{N_{f}}\right)$ | Microbial inactivation | log units | Pasteurization / sterilization targets |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log(N_{0}/N)}$ | Decimal reduction time | minutes (at T) | Sterilization validation |
| 6 | $12D\ Concept$ | Botulinum cook (121.1°C / 3 min) | log units | Low-acid canned food safety |
| 7 | $Moisture\ Content\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | Water content by mass | % | Compositional analysis |
| 8 | $Acceptance\ Limit = \dfrac{Specification\ value}{Safety\ Factor}$ | Regulatory threshold | varies | Contaminant regulation |
| 9 | $F_{0} = 10^{\,(T - 121.1)/z} \cdot \Delta t$ | Sterilization value | minutes | Canned food lethality |
| 10 | $Quality\ Grade = f(Sensory, Chemical, Microbial)$ | Composite grading | Grade A/B/C | AGMARK, Codex grading |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Caution:** When writing inline math (e.g., $a_{w}$) in tables, never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol for absolute value, as it breaks the table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Food classification systems directly impact:

- **HACCP Plan Design:** Identifying **Critical Control Points (CCPs)** requires knowing whether a food is high-acid or low-acid.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Perishable foods need refrigeration at **0–4°C**; non-perishables can be stored at ambient temperature.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FSSAI's Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations rely on classification for setting limits.
- **Trade and Labeling:** Codex classification enables international trade through harmonized standards.
- **Product Development:** Reformulation of processed foods requires knowledge of additive functional classes and their synergistic or antagonistic interactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Water Activity from Fundamental Thermodynamics

The concept of water activity is derived from classical thermodynamics applied to food systems.

**Step 1: Define the Chemical Potential of Water in a Food**

The chemical potential of water in a food matrix is given by:

$$\mu_{w} = \mu_{w}^{\circ} + R T \ln(a_{w})$$

Where:
- $\mu_{w}$ = chemical potential of water in food (J/mol)
- $\mu_{w}^{\circ}$ = chemical potential of pure water at the same temperature (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $a_{w}$ = water activity (dimensionless)

**Step 2: Equilibrium Condition with Surroundings**

At equilibrium between the food and its surrounding atmosphere, the chemical potential of water in the food equals that of water vapor in the headspace:

$$\mu_{w}^{food} = \mu_{w}^{vapor}$$

**Step 3: Express Vapor Phase Chemical Potential**

For the water vapor (treated as an ideal gas):

$$\mu_{w}^{vapor} = \mu_{w}^{\circ} + R T \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{w}}{P_{w}^{\circ}}\right)$$

Where:
- $P_{w}$ = partial pressure of water vapor above the food (Pa)
- $P_{w}^{\circ}$ = saturation vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature (Pa)

**Step 4: Equate and Simplify**

Equating the two expressions:

$$\mu_{w}^{\circ} + R T \ln(a_{w}) = \mu_{w}^{\circ} + R T \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{w}}{P_{w}^{\circ}}\right)$$

Canceling the reference terms on both sides:

$$R T \ln(a_{w}) = R T \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{w}}{P_{w}^{\circ}}\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $R T$:

$$\ln(a_{w}) = \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{w}}{P_{w}^{\circ}}\right)$$

**Step 5: Final Definition**

Taking the exponential of both sides:

$$a_{w} = \dfrac{P_{w}}{P_{w}^{\circ}}$$

This is the **operational definition** of water activity as the ratio of partial vapor pressure above the food to the saturation vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Required Sterilization Time Using D and z Values

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark style):**
A canned food has an initial spore load of *Clostridium botulinum* of $N_{0} = 1 \times 10^{4}$ spores per can. The decimal reduction time (D value) at 121.1°C is **D = 0.25 minutes**. The target is a **12D reduction** (the botulinum cook). Calculate the required processing time.

**Step 1: Define the D value concept**

The D value is the time required at a specific temperature to reduce the microbial population by **90% (1 log cycle)**:

$$D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10}(N_{0}/N)}$$

Where:
- $D$ = decimal reduction time (minutes)
- $t$ = heating time (minutes)
- $N_{0}$ = initial count
- $N$ = final count after time $t$

**Step 2: Set up the 12D requirement**

A 12D process reduces the population by 12 log cycles:

$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_{0}}{N}\right) = 12$$

**Step 3: Solve for time using the D value equation**

Rearranging the D value equation:

$$t = D \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_{0}}{N}\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$t = 0.25 \times 12$$

**Step 4: Final calculation**

$$t = 3.0 \text{ minutes}$$

This matches the **classical botulinum cook**: 3 minutes at 121.1°C. The required processing time for this canned food at 121.1°C is **3 minutes**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Classifications to Practical Applications

The following matrix links each classification system to its **industrial application**, **regulatory anchor**, and **typical KTU exam weightage**.

| Classification System | Industrial Application | Regulatory Anchor | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perishability (Perishable / Semi / Non) | Cold chain design, retail display | FSSAI Storage Conditions | **Very High** |
| pH (High / Medium / Low Acid) | Thermal process design, pathogen control | FDA 21 CFR 113, Codex | **Very High** |
| Water Activity (a<sub>w</sub>) | Shelf life prediction, drying technology | Codex, ASTM | **High** |
| Food Hazards (Bio / Chem / Phys) | HACCP plan, risk assessment | WHO, Codex, FSSAI | **Very High** |
| Additives (Functional class) | Product formulation, labeling | FSSAI, Codex GSFA | **High** |
| Quality Attributes (Identity/Accept/Keep) | Specification setting, grading | ISO 9001, AGMARK | **Moderate** |
| Grades (A/B/C or Special/Standard/General) | Trade, market price | AGMARK, Codex | **Moderate** |

### 3.4 Decision Algorithm: Classifying a New Food Product

**Step 1:** Measure the **pH** → categorize as High / Medium / Low Acid.
**Step 2:** Measure the **a<sub>w</sub>** → identify which microbial groups can grow.
**Step 3:** Determine the **origin** (plant, animal, fungal, synthetic).
**Step 4:** Evaluate the **processing history** (raw, pasteurized, sterilized, dried).
**Step 5:** Assess **perishability** based on combined pH + a<sub>w</sub> + storage.
**Step 6:** Identify **inherent hazards** (biological, chemical, physical).
**Step 7:** Assign a **grade** based on sensory + chemical + microbial criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Classify XYZ food," do not just list one category. Demonstrate the ability to **cross-classify** — e.g., "Fresh milk is **perishable**, **low-acid** (pH ≈ 6.7), **high a<sub>w</sub>** (≈ 0.99), of **animal origin**, and harbors **biological hazards** (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*)." This demonstrates integrated knowledge and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram (Foods)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Any Food Item]) --> A{Origin?}
    A -->|Plant| B1[Fruits, Vegetables, Cereals, Legumes, Nuts]
    A -->|Animal| B2[Meat, Fish, Poultry, Milk, Eggs, Honey]
    A -->|Fungal| B3[Mushrooms, Yeast, Fermented Products]
    A -->|Mineral/Synthetic| B4[Water, Salt, Sugar, Additives]

    B1 --> C{pH Check}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C -->|pH less than 3.7| D1[HIGH ACID FOODS]
    C -->|pH 3.7 to 4.5| D2[ACID FOODS]
    C -->|pH 4.5 to 4.6| D3[MEDIUM ACID FOODS]
    C -->|pH greater than 4.6| D4[LOW ACID FOODS]

    D1 --> E{Aw Check}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E -->|Aw 0.95 to 1.00| F1[HIGH MOISTURE - Perishable]
    E -->|Aw 0.60 to 0.95| F2[INTERMEDIATE - Semi Stable]
    E -->|Aw less than 0.60| F3[LOW MOISTURE - Stable Shelf]

    F1 --> G{Hazard Type?}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G -->|Biological| H1[Pathogens Parasites Toxins]
    G -->|Chemical| H2[Pesticides Heavy Metals Additives]

    H1 --> I[Final Classification + Grade Assignment]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J([Specifying Standards for HACCP, FSSAI, AGMARK])
```

### 4.2 Hazard Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[FOOD HAZARDS] --> BIO[BIOLOGICAL HAZARDS]
    H --> CHEM[CHEMICAL HAZARDS]

    BIO --> B1[Pathogenic Bacteria]
    BIO --> B2[Viruses]
    BIO --> B3[Parasites]

    BIO --> B5[Toxigenic Fungi]

    B1 --> B1A[Salmonella, E coli, Listeria, Clostridium botulinum]
    B2 --> B2A[Hepatitis A, Norovirus]
    B3 --> B3A[Taenia, Giardia, Trichinella]
    B4 --> B4A[BSE Agent in Beef]
    B5 --> B5A[Aspergillus producing Aflatoxin]

    CHEM --> C1[Natural Toxins]
    CHEM --> C2[Environmental Contaminants]
    CHEM --> C3[Process Induced]
    CHEM --> C4[Allergens]

    C1 --> C1A[Cyanogenic Glycosides, Solanine, Histamine]
    C2 --> C2A[Pesticides, Heavy Metals, Antibiotics]
    C3 --> C3A[PAH from Smoking, Acrylamide from Frying]
    C4 --> C4A[Big 8 Allergens: Milk, Egg, Fish, Shellfish, Nuts, Peanut, Wheat, Soy]

    PHYS --> P1[Glass]

    PHYS --> P4[Plastic and Stone]

```

### 4.3 Additive Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A --> G[Flavor Enhancers]

    A --> J[Anticaking Agents]

    B --> B1[Sodium Benzoate, BHA, BHT, Sorbates]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
