The **Food Safety and Standards (Advertising and Claims) Regulations, 2018** is the principal Indian regulation governing health claims. The operational logic is as follows:

- **Step 1.1:** Identify the claim category (Nutrient / Health / DRRC).
- **Step 1.2:** Determine if the claim falls under the **permitted list** issued by FSSAI from time to time.
- **Step 1.3:** If permitted, ensure the food complies with the **minimum composition threshold** (e.g., ≥15% RDA for "Source of" claims).
- **Step 1.4:** Submit the **product dossier** to FSSAI, including:
  - Product formulation and composition analysis.
  - Scientific literature review (preferably meta-analyses).
  - Clinical trial data (for DRRC).
  - Proposed label artwork.
- **Step 1.5:** Receive approval / rejection within **90–120 days**.

### Step 2: Understanding the EU (EFSA) Framework

Under **Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006**, the European Commission authorizes claims based on EFSA's scientific opinion. The process flow:

$$\text{Claim Draft} \rightarrow \text{EFSA Scientific Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{European Commission Decision} \rightarrow \text{Authorised Claim Register}$$

A landmark case: The **beta-glucan / oat fibre claim** was approved by EFSA, stating that "**Oat beta-glucan has been shown to lower/reduce blood cholesterol. High cholesterol is a risk factor in the development of coronary heart disease.**"

### Step 3: Understanding the US FDA Framework

The US FDA operates under **21 CFR § 101.14** for health claims. Claims are classified by the level of evidence:

- **Authorized Health Claims** — FDA pre-approved (e.g., "Calcium and Vitamin D reduce osteoporosis risk").
- **Qualified Health Claims** — Based on less conclusive evidence, requiring a disclaimer (e.g., "Some scientific evidence suggests...").

$$\text{Authorized Claims} = \text{High-quality evidence (Grade I)}$$

$$\text{Qualified Claims} = \text{Emerging evidence (Grade II–III)}$$

The level of scientific evidence is graded using the **FDA Evidence-Based Review System** as follows:

$$\text{Evidence Grade} = f(\text{Number of RCTs}, \text{Effect Size}, \text{Consistency}, \text{Biological Plausibility})$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Substantiation Criteria (Codex CAC/GL 23-1997, Rev. 2004)

For a health claim to be approved under the Codex model, the following conditions must be satisfied (each derived step from the Codex guideline):

### Logical Derivation of Claim Eligibility:

**Step 1 — Claim Identification:**
A claim must explicitly state the **food, food component, health effect, and target population**.

$$\text{Claim} = \{F, C, E, P\}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Food or category of food
- $C$ = Specific component of the food
- $E$ = Specific health effect or disease risk
- $P$ = Target population (general or subgroup)

**Step 2 — Evidence Quality Assessment:**
The evidence must be evaluated using a hierarchy:

$$\text{RCTs (Level I)} > \text{Cohort Studies (Level II)} > \text{Case-Control (Level III)} > \text{Cross-Sectional (Level IV)} > \text{Expert Opinion (Level V)}$$

**Step 3 — Risk-Benefit Analysis:**
The claim should not encourage excessive consumption of a food. If a food naturally contains high sugar but is being claimed for "natural antioxidants", the claim should not promote overconsumption.

$$\text{Consumer Protection Index (CPI)} = \frac{\text{Evidence Strength} \times \text{Health Benefit}}{\text{Risk of Overconsumption}}$$

**Step 4 — Label Compliance:**
The claim must include:
- A statement of the quantity of the food needed to achieve the claimed effect.
- A reference to the importance of a varied and balanced diet.
- A statement of any population groups for whom the food is contraindicated.

---

## 3.3 Prohibited Claims Under FSSAI Regulations

The following claims are explicitly **prohibited** on food labels in India (per FSSAI Advertising & Claims Regulations 2018):

1. Claims that a food has healing properties or can cure, treat, diagnose, or prevent any disease.
2. Claims stating that a particular food is a "guaranteed cure" or "100% effective".
3. Claims comparing the food to a drug or medicine.
4. Claims that imply dietary modification alone is sufficient to prevent disease.
5. Claims that disparage or misrepresent another food product.
6. Claims containing words like "tonic", "cure", "remedy" (reserved for drugs under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940).
7. Therapeutic claims for infant foods (Schedule XX of FSSAI Regulations).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Health Claims vs. Related Concepts

| Parameter | Nutrient Claim | Structure/Function Claim | Disease Risk Reduction Claim |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference to disease** | No | No | Yes (explicit) |
| **Level of evidence required** | Low (composition only) | Moderate | High (clinical trials) |
| **Regulatory approval needed** | Yes (if specified in regulations) | Usually self-regulatory | Yes (mandatory pre-approval) |
| **Example** | "Low in saturated fat" | "Calcium supports bone health" | "Plant sterols reduce heart disease risk" |
| **Governing body** | FSSAI / FDA | FDA (DSHEA) / Codex | EFSA / FDA / FSSAI |
| **Cost of substantiation** | Low | Moderate | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Food Claims

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Claim] --> B{Describes content?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Nutrient Claim]
    B -->|No| D{References disease?}
    C --> C1[Free From Claim]
    C --> C2[Source / High / Low Claim]
    C --> C3[Reduced / Lite Claim]
    D -->|Yes| E[Disease Risk Reduction Claim]
    D -->|No| F[Structure Function Claim]
    E --> E1[Cardiovascular Risk]
    E --> E2[Cancer Risk]
    E --> E3[Diabetes Risk]
    F --> F1[Immune Support]
    F --> F2[Digestive Health]
    F --> F3[Cognitive Function]
    E1 --> G[FSSAI / EFSA Pre Approval Needed]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> H[Generally Accepted Scientific Evidence]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
```

## 4.2 Approval Workflow for Health Claims (FSSAI / EFSA Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applicant Industry] --> B[Submit Dossier]
    B --> C{FSSAI or EFSA Review}
    C --> D[Scientific Evaluation]
    D --> E{Evidence Sufficient?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Issue Approval Order]
    E -->|No| G[Rejection with Reasons]
    F --> H[Approved Claim Published in Register]
    H --> I[Manufacturer Uses Claim on Label]
    I --> J[Post Market Surveillance]
    J --> K{Adverse Effects Reported?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Withdraw or Modify Claim]
    K -->|No| M[Claim Remains Valid]
```

## 4.3 Substantiation Evidence Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evidence Pyramid for Health Claims] --> B[Level 1 Meta Analyses of RCTs]
    B --> C[Level 2 Randomized Controlled Trials]
    C --> D[Level 3 Cohort Studies]
    D --> E[Level 4 Case Control Studies]
    E --> F[Level 5 Cross Sectional Studies]
    F --> G[Level 6 Expert Opinion and Animal Studies]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The higher the level on the pyramid, the stronger the evidence base. EFSA and FSSAI strongly prefer Level 1–2 evidence for any Disease Risk Reduction Claim (DRRC).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Define "Health Claim" as per Codex Alimentarius guidelines. Differentiate it from a "Structure/Function Claim".**

**Model Answer:**

A **Health Claim** is defined by the Codex Alimentarius (CAC/GL 23-1997, Rev. 2004) as *any representation that states, suggests, or implies a relationship between a food, a food component, or the constituents of a food, and human health*.

**Difference from a Structure/Function Claim:**

| Basis | Health Claim | Structure/Function Claim |
|---|---|---|
| **Disease reference** | May reference disease risk reduction | Does NOT reference any disease |
| **Example** | "Calcium reduces osteoporosis risk" | "Calcium builds strong bones" |
| **Evidence requirement** | Higher (clinical trials needed) | Moderate (Generally Accepted Scientific Evidence) |
| **Regulatory scrutiny** | High | Lower |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation table)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: List any THREE prohibited health claims on food labels as per FSSAI (Advertising and Claims) Regulations, 2018.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Claims that a food product can cure, treat, diagnose, or prevent any specific disease (e.g., "Cures diabetes", "Prevents cancer").
2. Claims using drug-related terminology such as "tonic", "medicinal", "remedy", "miracle cure".
3. Claims comparing a food to a drug or implying the food has pharmacological effects.

*(1 mark each, total 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: (a) Classify the different types of health claims used on food labels. Explain each with a suitable example.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Describe the regulatory framework for health claims in India under FSSAI. What are the substantiation requirements?** *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Health Claims (7 marks):**

Health claims on food labels are broadly classified into the following categories:

**1. Nutrient Content Claims (2 marks):**
These describe the level of a nutrient in the food. Examples: "Source of fibre", "Low in fat", "Cholesterol-free". Governed under FSSAI's threshold-based criteria.

**2. General Health Claims (2 marks):**
These describe the role of a nutrient in maintaining general health without referring to a specific disease. Example: "Vitamin C supports immunity." Required evidence is Generally Accepted Scientific Evidence (GASE).

**3. Disease Risk Reduction Claims (DRRC) (2 marks):**
These explicitly link a food component to reduced risk of a specific disease. Example: "Plant sterols reduce blood cholesterol and the risk of coronary heart disease." Requires high-quality clinical evidence and pre-market approval from FSSAI/EFSA.

**4. Structure/Function Claims (1 mark):**
Describe the effect on normal body structure or function. Example: "Calcium builds strong bones." No disease reference allowed.

---

**(b) FSSAI Regulatory Framework & Substantiation (7 marks):**

The principal regulation is the **Food Safety and Standards (Advertising and Claims) Regulations, 2018**.

**Key Provisions (3 marks):**
- FSSAI is the apex authority for approving claims.
- Claims must be based on valid scientific evidence.
- Prohibited claims are explicitly listed in the regulations.
- Claims must not be misleading.

**Substantiation Requirements (4 marks):**
- **Compositional analysis:** Lab testing to verify nutrient content.
- **Scientific literature review:** Published peer-reviewed studies supporting the claim.
- **Clinical trial data:** For DRRC, randomized controlled trials with adequate sample size and statistical power.
- **Dose-response data:** The minimum effective dose required to achieve the claimed effect.
- **Population targeting:** Specification of the target consumer group (e.g., adults, diabetics, hypertensive patients).

The complete dossier is reviewed by FSSAI, and approval typically takes **90–120 days**. Post-approval, the manufacturer must conduct **post-market surveillance** to ensure no adverse effects are reported.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a) Explain the Codex Alimentarius (CAC/GL 23-1997) framework for evaluating health claims on foods.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Differentiate between the FDA (US) and EFSA (Europe) approaches to authorizing health claims.** *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution:

**(a) Codex Alimentarius Framework (7 marks):**

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission's CAC/GL 23-1997 (Rev. 2004)** provides the international reference framework for health claims. It establishes:

**1. Claim Definition (1 mark):** A claim that states/implies a relationship between food (or component) and human health.

**2. Claim Categorization (2 marks):**
- Nutrient content claims
- Comparative claims
- Functional claims
- Disease risk reduction claims

**3. Conditions for Acceptable Claims (2 marks):**
- Based on sound scientific evidence.
- Does not encourage overconsumption.
- Clearly communicates the required quantity to achieve the effect.
- References the importance of a varied diet.

**4. Evidence Hierarchy (2 marks):** The claim should be supported by the highest available level of evidence, ideally **Level I (meta-analyses of RCTs)** or **Level II (individual RCTs)**.

---

**(b) FDA vs EFSA — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Basis | FDA (US FDA) | EFSA (EU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal basis** | 21 CFR § 101.14; DSHEA 1994 | Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006 |
| **Pre-approval** | Required for Authorized Health Claims; optional for Qualified Claims | Mandatory pre-approval for all health claims |
| **Evidence thresholds** | Significant Scientific Agreement (SSA) for Authorized Claims | Generally Accepted Scientific Evidence (GASE) |
| **Qualified claims** | Allowed with disclaimer | Not permitted under EU law |
| **Structure/Function claims** | Permitted (post-DSHEA) for supplements | Not permitted under EU regulation |
| **Disease claims** | Allowed (with pre-approval) | Allowed only with EFSA approval |
| **Number of approved claims** | ~25 Authorized + several Qualified | ~240+ on EU Register |

*(Approx. 4 marks for the table, 3 marks for narrative explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> A common mistake students make is **confusing Structure/Function claims with Disease Risk Reduction claims**. If a student writes *"Calcium prevents osteoporosis"* instead of *"Calcium reduces the risk of osteoporosis"*, marks are deducted because "prevents" implies a guaranteed cure, which is a **prohibited claim**. Always use hedging language: *reduces the risk of, supports, contributes to*. Additionally, do not list nutrient content claims as examples of health claims — they are a **separate category** in the FSSAI framework.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Health Claim Definition:** Any representation linking food (or its component) to human health, including disease risk reduction. (Codex CAC/GL 23-1997, Rev. 2004)
- **Three Major Categories:** Nutrient Claims, Structure/Function Claims, and Disease Risk Reduction Claims (DRRC).
- **Indian Regulation:** FSSAI (Advertising and Claims) Regulations, 2018 governs all claims.
- **Global Bodies:** Codex (international), FDA (US), EFSA (EU).
- **"Source of" threshold:** ≥15% of RDA per serving; **"High in"** ≥30% of RDA.
- **Plant sterol/stanol dose:** ≥1.5 g/day for blood cholesterol claims (EFSA-approved).
- **Prohibited claims:** Any claim of cure, treatment, or prevention of specific diseases; use of drug terminology like "tonic" or "medicinal".
- **Substantiation:** Requires compositional analysis + peer-reviewed scientific literature + clinical trials (for DRRC).
- **Evidence hierarchy:** RCTs > Cohort > Case-Control > Cross-Sectional > Expert Opinion.
- **Post-market surveillance:** Mandatory after claim approval to monitor adverse effects.
- **Consumer Protection Index (CPI)** = (Evidence Strength × Health Benefit) ÷ Risk of Overconsumption.
- **Always use hedging language** in your answers: *reduces risk, supports, contributes to* — NEVER *cures, prevents, guarantees*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mandatory Labeling Provision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mandatory Labeling Provision # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Labeling Provision (KTU 2024 Definition)** > *Mandatory Labeling Provision* refers to the **legally binding statutory requirements** prescribed under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI)** and the **Food Safety and Standards (Packaging and Labelling) Regulations, 2011** (as amended up to 2024), which mandate that every pre-packaged food item sold in the Indian market **must display a defined set of declarations** on its label, in a conspicuous and legible manner, before it can be offered for retail sale. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic is anchored to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply food laws, regulations, and quality management systems in ensuring food safety and compliance."* The key governing framework includes: - **FSS Act, 2006** (Parent Legislation) - **FSS (Packaging and Labelling) Regulations, 2011** - **Codex Alimentarius General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985, Rev. 2010)** - **Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > As per the PEAGT633 Module 2 syllabus, students must be able to: *"Explain the mandatory labeling requirements as per FSSAI and Codex standards, identify the 17+ mandatory declarations, calculate nutritional information, and interpret labeling provisions for different food categories."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are about to buy a medicine. Before consuming it, you check the strip for the **drug name**, **dosage**, **expiry date**, **manufacturer**, and **side effects**. The government **mandates** this information because your health depends on it. Now apply the **same logic** to food. Food is something we (or our families) consume daily, sometimes multiple times. Without labeling, you would not know: - What **ingredients** are inside (especially allergens like nuts or gluten)? - When does it **expire** (risk of food poisoning)? - Is it **vegetarian or non-vegetarian** (religious/cultural choice)? - What is the **nutritional value** (for diabetics, dieters, athletes)? - Who is **legally responsible** if something goes wrong (accountability)? > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a food label as the **"Aadhaar Card + Medical Report + Passport"** of a food product — it proves **identity** (what it is), **health status** (nutritional value, expiry), and **origin** (where it came from, who made it). Just as you cannot board a flight without a passport, a food product **cannot legally be sold** in India without a compliant label. ## 1.3 Core Objectives of Mandatory Labeling | S.No | Objective | Practical Significance | |:----:|:----------|:-----------------------| | 1 | **Consumer Protection** | Prevents fraud, adulteration, and misbranding | | 2 | **Informed Choice** | Allows consumers to compare products on nutrition | | 3 | **Allergen Safety** | Warns consumers with food hypersensitivities | | 4 | **Traceability** | Enables batch recall in case of contamination | | 5 | **Regulatory Compliance** | Fulfills statutory requirements under FSSAI | | 6 | **Fair Trade** | Standardizes declarations across all manufacturers | --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Statutory Authority:** > **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** is the apex body under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of India, empowered under **Section 23 of the FSS Act, 2006** to enforce packaging and labelling regulations. The **FSSAI Logo** (a stylized "S" with a green leaf and orange dot) on every label signifies regulatory approval. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 18 Mandatory Declarations on a Pre-Packaged Food Label As per the **FSS (Packaging and Labelling) Regulations, 2011 (Reg. 4)**, every pre-packaged food must carry the following **18 mandatory declarations** on the label: ### **Block A: Identity & Product Information** 1. **Name of the Food** — Must be specific (e.g., "Tomato Ketchup" not just "Sauce"). 2. **List of Ingredients** — In descending order of weight (by composition). 3. **Declaration of Veg or Non-Veg Symbol** — 🟢 Green dot in green square for VEG; 🔴 Brown dot in brown square for NON-VEG. 4. **Declaration regarding Food Additives** — Both functional class name and specific name (or INS number). ### **Block B: Manufacturer & Origin Information** 5. **Name and Complete Address of the Manufacturer/Packer/Marketer.** 6. **Country of Origin** — Mandatory for imported foods. 7. **FSSAI Logo and License Number** — 14-digit numeric license (e.g., `10012011002345`). 8. **Net Quantity** — By weight (grams/kg), volume (ml/L), or number (count). ### **Block C: Time & Traceability Markers** 9. **Date of Manufacture** (DD/MM/YY). 10. **Date of Expiry / Best Before** (DD/MM/YY). 11. **Batch/Lot Number** — For traceability and recall. 12. **Storage Conditions** — "Store in cool & dry place," "Refrigerate after opening," etc. ### **Block D: Nutritional & Health Declarations** 13. **Nutritional Information** — Energy (kcal), Protein, Carbohydrate, Fat (saturated/trans), Sugar, Sodium — per 100g or 100ml or per serving. 14. **Declaration of Allergens** — Mandatory if the product contains cereals with gluten, crustacea, eggs, fish, peanuts, soybeans, milk, nuts, etc. 15. **Best Before Date with Storage Instructions** — For shelf-stable products. ### **Block E: Special & Statutory Marks** 16. **Consumer Care Details** — Phone, email, or website for complaints. 17. **Instructions for Use** — Especially for infant foods, health supplements, etc. 18. **Other Statutory Warnings** — "Smoking is injurious to health," "Alcohol consumption is injurious," etc. ## 2.2 Logical Breakdown: The "How & Why" of Each Declaration ### **Why "Descending Order of Ingredients"?** The ingredient present in the **highest quantity** is listed **first**. This follows the **Codex Alimentarius principle** (Section 4.2 of CXS 1-1985). For example, in a "Fruit Juice" that is mostly water + sugar + 10% fruit, the label reads: *"Water, Sugar, Fruit Pulp (10%), Acidity Regulator (INS 330)..."* This protects consumers from being misled. ### **Why the Veg/Non-Veg Symbol?** Introduced under the **Food Safety and Standards (Packaging and Labelling) Second Amendment Regulations, 2018**, this addresses **religious and cultural sensitivities** of the Indian consumer base, particularly ensuring that lacto-vegetarians can confidently identify vegetarian products. > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule of FSSAI Labeling:** > *"The label shall not contain any statement, claim, or imagery that is false, misleading, or deceptive, or that is likely to create an erroneous impression regarding the product's character, quality, composition, or origin."* — Regulation 3(1)(a), FSS (P&L) Regulations, 2011. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Since food labeling involves quantitative calculations (especially for **Nutritional Information**), here is the consolidated formula sheet: ### **Table 2.1: Nutritional Calculation Formulas** | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes | |:----------|:--------|:----:|:------| | **Energy from Protein** | $E_{prot} = m_{prot} \times 4$ | kcal | 1g protein = **4 kcal** | | **Energy from Carbohydrate** | $E_{carb} = m_{carb} \times 4$ | kcal | 1g carb = **4 kcal** | | **Energy from Fat** | $E_{fat} = m_{fat} \times 9$ | kcal | 1g fat = **9 kcal** | | **Total Energy** | $E_{total} = E_{prot} + E_{carb} + E_{fat}$ | kcal | Sum of macronutrients | | **% RDA Calculation** | $\%RDA = \dfrac{Nutrient\,per\,serving}{RDA\,value} \times 100$ | % | Based on 2000 kcal diet | | **Per Serving Conversion** | $N_{serving} = \dfrac{N_{100g} \times M_{serving}}{100}$ | g or mg | $M_{serving}$ = serving size in g | | **Net Quantity Tolerance (Legal Metrology)** | $\delta_{net} = \pm \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{N_{units}}} \times 100$ | % | For $N_{units} \leq 50$; $N_{units} \geq 50 \Rightarrow \delta = \pm 1\%$ | | **Shelf-Life Estimation** | $SL = D_{exp} - D_{mfg}$ | Days/Months | As printed on label | ### **Table 2.2: Standard RDA Reference Values (ICMR-NIN, 2020)** | Nutrient | Adult Male (Sedentary) | Adult Female (Sedentary) | |:---------|:----------------------:|:------------------------:| | Energy | **2000 kcal/day** | **1600 kcal/day** | | Protein | **60 g/day** | **55 g/day** | | Total Fat | **67 g/day** | **54 g/day** | | Carbohydrate | **300 g/day** | **240 g/day** | | Sodium | **2000 mg/day** | **2000 mg/day** | ### **Table 2.3: Font Size & Visibility Rules** | Declaration | Minimum Font Size | |:------------|:-----------------:| | Name of food, net qty, FSSAI logo | **3 mm** height | | Other mandatory declarations | **2 mm** height | | Total label area | Min **100 cm²** for retail packs | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In food technology practice, mandatory labeling is applied in: - **Nutraceutical industry** — for health supplements, % RDA calculation is critical. - **Export-quality food processing** — Codex labeling governs international trade. - **Quality assurance (QA) labs** — verification of label claims against actual lab analysis. - **Packaging line automation** — inkjet printers must be calibrated to print **DD/MM/YY format** expiry at **3 mm font height**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Connection:** > For B.Tech Food Technology students, mastering labeling calculations is essential for roles in **QA/QC departments, regulatory affairs, and product development**. A common KTU exam question tests your ability to compute **per-serving nutritional values** from per-100g data — a real-world skill in **product label design**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Calculating Nutritional Information on a Label ### **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question Type):** A food manufacturer produces a **"Crunchy Oat Cookies"** product. The lab analysis of 100 g of cookies yields the following composition: - **Protein** = 8.0 g - **Carbohydrate** (total) = 62.0 g (of which **Sugar** = 22.0 g) - **Fat** (total) = 18.0 g (of which **Saturated Fat** = 6.5 g, **Trans Fat** = 0.4 g) - **Sodium** = 320 mg - **Serving Size** declared on label = 30 g - **Number of servings per pack** = 1 pack = 100 g (≈ 3.33 servings) **Task:** Calculate the **per 100 g** and **per serving (30 g)** nutritional declarations to be printed on the front label. --- ### **Step 1: Calculate Energy from Each Macronutrient** Using the **Atwater factors** (standard FSSAI accepted values): Energy from Protein: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{prot} &= m_{prot} \times 4 \,\, \text{kcal/g} \\ &= 8.0 \times 4 \\ &= 32.0 \,\, \text{kcal} \end{aligned} $$ Energy from Carbohydrate: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{carb} &= m_{carb} \times 4 \,\, \text{kcal/g} \\ &= 62.0 \times 4 \\ &= 248.0 \,\, \text{kcal} \end{aligned} $$ Energy from Fat: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{fat} &= m_{fat} \times 9 \,\, \text{kcal/g} \\ &= 18.0 \times 9 \\ &= 162.0 \,\, \text{kcal} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Step 2: Calculate Total Energy per 100 g** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{total} &= E_{prot} + E_{carb} + E_{fat} \\ &= 32.0 + 248.0 + 162.0 \\ &= 442.0 \,\, \text{kcal} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Step 3: Convert All Nutrients to Per-Serving Basis (30 g)** Using the formula $N_{serving} = \dfrac{N_{100g} \times M_{serving}}{100}$: Protein per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{serving} &= \dfrac{8.0 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 2.4 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Carbohydrate per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{serving} &= \dfrac{62.0 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 18.6 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Sugar per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{serving} &= \dfrac{22.0 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 6.6 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Fat per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{serving} &= \dfrac{18.0 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 5.4 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Saturated Fat per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} SF_{serving} &= \dfrac{6.5 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 1.95 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Trans Fat per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} TF_{serving} &= \dfrac{0.4 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 0.12 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ Sodium per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} Na_{serving} &= \dfrac{320 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 96 \,\, \text{mg} \end{aligned} $$ Energy per serving: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{serving} &= \dfrac{442 \times 30}{100} \\ &= 132.6 \,\, \text{kcal} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Step 4: Calculate % RDA for a Few Key Nutrients** Using the standard RDA for an adult (2000 kcal diet): % RDA of Protein: $$ \begin{aligned} \%RDA_{prot} &= \dfrac{2.4}{60} \times 100 \\ &= 4.0 \,\% \end{aligned} $$ % RDA of Total Fat: $$ \begin{aligned} \%RDA_{fat} &= \dfrac{5.4}{67} \times 100 \\ &= 8.06 \,\% \end{aligned} $$ % RDA of Sodium: $$ \begin{aligned} \%RDA_{Na} &= \dfrac{96}{2000} \times 100 \\ &= 4.8 \,\% \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Step 5: Final Label Declaration (As It Appears on Pack)** > **NUTRITIONAL INFORMATION (Per 100 g / Per Serving of 30 g)** | Nutrient | Per 100 g | Per Serving (30 g) | % RDA per serving | |:---------|:---------:|:-------------------:|:------------------:| | Energy | 442 kcal | 132.6 kcal | 6.6% | | Protein | 8.0 g | 2.4 g | 4.0% | | Carbohydrate | 62.0 g | 18.6 g | — | | — of which Sugars | 22.0 g | 6.6 g | — | | Total Fat | 18.0 g | 5.4 g | 8.06% | | — Saturated Fat | 6.5 g | 1.95 g | — | | — Trans Fat | 0.4 g | 0.12 g | — | | Sodium | 320 mg | 96 mg | 4.8% | --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Special Labeling Provisions for Different Food Categories | Food Category | Special Labeling Requirement | FSSAI Reference | |:--------------|:-----------------------------|:----------------| | **Packaged Drinking Water** | BIS mark mandatory, source of water, TDS | FSS (Packaged Drinking Water) Reg., 2018 | | **Infant Milk Substitute** | "Mother's milk is best" warning, no infant image | Regulation 5(2), IMS Reg., 2019 | | **Edible Oils** | Cold-pressed / Refined declaration, free fatty acid % | FSS (Oil & Fat) Reg., 2021 | | **Organic Food** | Jaivik Bharat Logo (Green), Certifying agency | NPOP + FSS (Organic Foods) Reg., 2017 | | **Genetically Modified Food** | "Contains GMO/Ingredients from GMO" — mandatory | FSS (GM Foods) Reg., 2023 (Draft) | | **Fortified Food** | "+F" Logo (Blue), fortificant name and level | FSS (Fortification) Reg., 2018 | | **Alcoholic Beverages** | "Alcohol consumption is injurious to health" | Cable TV Networks Reg., 1994 | | **Meat & Poultry** | Brown non-veg symbol mandatory | FSS (P&L) 2nd Amend., 2018 | | **Health Supplements** | "Not for medicinal use", % RDA for each nutrient | FSS (Nutraceuticals) Reg., 2016 | | **Pre-packaged Sweets** | Veg/Non-veg symbol, "Contains artificial color" if applicable | FSS (P&L) Reg., 2011 | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Shelf-Life and Date Coding Calculation ### **Problem:** A biscuit is manufactured on **15 March 2024**. The company has determined through accelerated stability studies (at 40°C/75% RH) that the product has a shelf life of **9 months**. Calculate the **"Best Before"** date to be printed on the label. ### **Step 1: Identify Manufacturing Date** $$ D_{mfg} = 15/03/2024 $$ ### **Step 2: Add Shelf Life** $$ D_{exp} = D_{mfg} + SL $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{exp} &= 15/03/2024 + 9 \,\, \text{months} \\ &= 15/12/2024 \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 3: Print Format** $$ \text{Best Before: 14/12/2024} $$ > [!NOTE] > **FSSAI Convention:** The "Best Before" date is generally printed as **one day prior** to the actual expiry to give a safety buffer, ensuring the product is consumed before genuine degradation begins. --- ## 3.4 Net Quantity Tolerance Verification (Legal Metrology) ### **Problem:** A pack declares **Net Wt: 500 g**. A QA inspector weighs **36 individual packs** and wants to determine the permissible negative error allowed under the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011. ### **Step 1: Apply the Tolerance Formula** For packs of quantity between 100 g to 500 g, the tolerance is given by: $$ \delta_{net} = \pm \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{N_{units}}} $$ Wait — this formula applies when $N_{units}$ is the number of units in a sample. For **per-pack tolerance**: Per-pack permissible negative error (for 500 g pack): $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_{pack} &= \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{500}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{1.5}{22.36} \times 100 \\ &= 6.71\% \,\, \text{(capped at legal max)} \end{aligned} $$ For quantities between **100 g–5 kg**, the **Legal Metrology Rules prescribe a fixed tolerance** of: $$ \delta_{fixed} = \pm 1.5\% \,\, \text{of the declared quantity} $$ ### **Step 2: Calculate Minimum Allowable Weight** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{min} &= 500 - (500 \times \dfrac{1.5}{100}) \\ &= 500 - 7.5 \\ &= 492.5 \,\, \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 3: Conclusion** Any pack weighing **less than 492.5 g** is in violation of Legal Metrology Rules and is liable for prosecution under the **Legal Metrology Act, 2009**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mandatory Label Elements ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mandatory Food Label

List any FOUR mandatory declarations required on the label of a pre-packaged food product.** #### **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A **"Pre-packaged food"** means any food that is **placed in a package of any nature** (including wrappers, containers, bottles, tins, or any other form) **in such a manner that the consumer cannot change the quantity, composition, or nature of the contents** without opening or changing the package, and where the contents are intended for direct sale to the consumer. **Four Mandatory Declarations:** 1. **Name of the food** — Specific and accurate. 2. **List of ingredients** — In descending order of weight. 3. **Net quantity** — By weight, volume, or number. 4. **Date of manufacture and expiry / Best before** — In DD/MM/YY format. 5. *(Bonus)* FSSAI logo and license number, Veg/Non-veg symbol. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` > **Explain the significance of the "Veg" and "Non-Veg" symbols as prescribed by FSSAI. State their colour, shape, and placement on the label.** #### **Model Answer:** **Significance:** - Mandated under **FSS (Packaging and Labelling) Second Amendment Regulations, 2018**. - Protects **religious and cultural sentiments** of vegetarian consumers in India. - Ensures **transparency** about animal-origin ingredients (meat, fish, eggs, dairy derivatives). **Specifications:** | Symbol | Color | Shape | Container Shape | |:------:|:------|:------|:----------------| | **VEG** 🟢 | **Green** | Circle | Within a **Green Square** border | | **NON-VEG** 🔴 | **Brown** | Triangle | Within a **Brown Square** border | **Placement:** Must be placed **near the brand name or product name** on the **Principal Display Panel (PDP)**, displayed prominently so as to be clearly visible. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` > **(a)** List the **mandatory declarations** to be made on the label of a pre-packaged food item as per the FSS (Packaging and Labelling) Regulations, 2011. Explain any **five** of them in detail. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A packet of **"Nutri-Bar Chocolate"** weighs 40 g. Its proximate composition per 100 g is: > Protein = 10 g, Carbohydrate = 60 g, Fat = 15 g, Sugar = 30 g, Sodium = 250 mg. > **Calculate:** > (i) Total energy per 100 g and per serving. **(3 Marks)** > (ii) % RDA of protein, fat, and sodium per serving (use ICMR 2000 kcal reference diet). **(4 Marks)** #### **Model Solution:** ### **Part (a): Mandatory Declarations (7 Marks)** The following declarations are **mandatory** under Regulation 4, FSS (P&L) Regulations, 2011: 1. **Name of the Food** — Must be specific; cannot use vague terms like "drink" when it is "soda." Should include the class/category. 2. **List of Ingredients** — Declared in **descending order of composition by weight**. For compound ingredients (e.g., "chocolate" containing cocoa, sugar, milk solids), the sub-ingredients must be declared unless exempted. 3. **Declaration of Additives** — Both the **functional class** (e.g., "Emulsifier") and the **specific name or INS number** (e.g., "INS 322 — Lecithin"). 4. **Net Quantity** — In **SI metric units**: grams (g), kilograms (kg), milliliters (ml), or liters (L). For solids: by weight; for liquids: by volume; for count: by number. 5. **Date of Manufacture / Best Before / Date of Expiry** — Must be in **DD/MM/YY format**. For products with shelf life: - **< 3 months:** Month & Year - **> 3 months:** Month & Year - **> 24 months:** No expiry date required (only manufacture date) 6. **Batch/Lot Number** — A unique identifier (alphanumeric) for traceability and recall. 7. **Name and Address of Manufacturer/Packer** — Complete postal address with pincode. 8. **FSSAI Logo and License Number** — 14-digit number prefixed with state code. 9. **Country of Origin** — Required for imported food products. 10. **Veg / Non-Veg Symbol** — Green dot or Brown dot, placed near the brand name. 11. **Nutritional Information** — Per 100 g or per 100 ml AND per serving. 12. **Allergen Declaration** — Mandatory mention of 8 major allergens. --- ### **Part (b): Nutritional Calculation (7 Marks)** **Given Data (per 100 g):** - Protein = 10 g - Carbohydrate = 60 g (of which Sugar = 30 g) - Fat = 15 g - Sodium = 250 mg - Serving Size = 40 g (one bar) ### **(i) Total Energy Calculation:** Using Atwater factors (4-4-9): Energy from Protein: $$ E_{prot} = 10 \times 4 = 40 \,\, \text{kcal} $$ Energy from Carbohydrate: $$ E_{carb} = 60 \times 4 = 240 \,\, \text{kcal} $$ Energy from Fat: $$ E_{fat} = 15 \times 9 = 135 \,\, \text{kcal} $$ Total Energy per 100 g: $$ E_{total} = 40 + 240 + 135 = 415 \,\, \text{kcal} $$ Total Energy per serving (40 g): $$ E_{serving} = \dfrac{415 \times 40}{100} = 166 \,\, \text{kcal} $$ ### **(ii) % RDA Calculation:** Per-serving nutrients: - Protein = $10 \times 40/100 = 4.0$ g - Fat = $15 \times 40/100 = 6.0$ g - Sodium = $250 \times 40/100 = 100$ mg % RDA Protein: $$ \%RDA_{prot} = \dfrac{4.0}{60} \times 100 = 6.67 \,\% $$ % RDA Fat: $$ \%RDA_{fat} = \dfrac{6.0}{67} \times 100 = 8.96 \,\% $$ % RDA Sodium: $$ \%RDA_{Na} = \dfrac{100}{2000} \times 100 = 5.0 \,\% $$ **Final Label Table:** | Nutrient | Per 100 g | Per Serving (40 g) | % RDA/serving | |:---------|:---------:|:------------------:|:-------------:| | Energy | 415 kcal | 166 kcal | 8.3% | | Protein | 10 g | 4.0 g | 6.67% | | Carbohydrate | 60 g | 24 g | — | | — Sugar | 30 g | 12 g | — | | Fat | 15 g | 6.0 g | 8.96% | | Sodium | 250 mg | 100 mg | 5.0% | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` > **(a)** Explain the **Codex Alimentarius General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985)**. Discuss its **scope, general principles, and key declarations**. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **specific labeling requirements** for the following food categories as per FSSAI: **(7 Marks)** > (i) Packaged Drinking Water > (ii) Infant Milk Substitute > (iii) Organic Foods > (iv) Genetically Modified Foods > (v) Edible Oils #### **Model Solution Outline:** ### **Part (a): Codex Alimentarius CXS 1-1985 (7 Marks)** **Origin & Authority:** - Adopted in **1985**, revised **2010** by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission** (joint body of FAO/WHO). - Applies to **all pre-packaged foods** offered for sale to the consumer. **Scope:** Covers food labeling for retail sale; excludes catering and bulk containers. **General Principles:** 1. **No misleading information** — Labels must not deceive the consumer. 2. **Clear and legible** — Easily understood by the consumer under normal conditions of purchase. 3. **Specific and accurate** — Generic names (e.g., "sauce") not allowed unless accompanied by the specific type. **Key Mandatory Declarations under CXS 1-1985:** 1. Name of the food 2. List of ingredients 3. Net contents (weight/volume) 4. Name and address of manufacturer 5. Country of origin 6. Lot identification 7. Date marking & storage instructions 8. Instructions for use (if needed) ### **Part (b): Specific Labeling Requirements (7 Marks)** | Category | Special Requirement | |:---------|:--------------------| | **(i) Packaged Drinking Water** | BIS certification (IS 14543), source of water (mineral/natural), TDS level, treatment method, BIS mark. | | **(ii) Infant Milk Substitute** | "Mother's milk is the best food for the baby" — **must** appear prominently. No pictures of infants/mothers. | | **(iii) Organic Foods** | **Jaivik Bharat Logo** (green with a leaf). Name and address of certifying agency. NPOP compliance. | | **(iv) GM Foods** | "Contains Genetically Modified Organisms" or "Produced from Genetically Modified Ingredients" — mandatory declaration. | | **(v) Edible Oils** | Declaration of **"Refined"** or **"Cold-Pressed"** or **"Virgin"**. Free Fatty Acid (FFA) %, Iodine Value, Saponification Value for specified oils. | --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout** > > **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing "Best Before" vs "Expiry Date":** "Best Before" is about **quality**, not safety. "Expiry/Use By" is about **safety**. Many students write them interchangeably — KTU examiners **deduct 1 full mark** for this. > > 2. **Wrong date format:** Writing "15/03/24" instead of "15/03/2024" or using MM/DD/YY (American format) is **incorrect** under FSSAI. > > 3. **Forgetting %RDA units:** % RDA is **per serving**, NOT per 100 g. Marks deducted if units are wrong. > > 4. **Missing Atwater factors:** Examiners expect you to memorize: Protein = 4 kcal/g, Carb = 4 kcal/g, Fat = 9 kcal/g. Forgetting fat = 9 is a common mistake (some students write 8.9 or 9.3). > > 5. **Ingredients order:** Some students list ingredients in alphabetical order or random order — this is **wrong**. Must be **descending by weight**. > > 6. **Symbol color confusion:** Writing "red dot for non-veg" — the correct color is **brown**. Examiners mark strictly. > > 7. **Ignoring "per 100 ml" for liquids:** Many students calculate nutrition for beverages per 100 g — wrong. FSSAI mandates **per 100 ml** for liquids. > > 8. **Not mentioning FSSAI License format:** Writing "FSSAI No." — the correct format is **"Lic. No."** followed by 14 digits, prefixed with state code (e.g., `10012011002345`). > > **Pro Tip:** Always quote the **Regulation Number** (e.g., Reg. 4, Reg. 5, Reg. 7) when answering FSSAI-related questions. Examiners **reward** such precision. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ✅ **Statutory Framework:** FSS Act, 2006 + FSS (Packaging & Labelling) Regulations, 2011 (amended 2018, 2021). ✅ **18 Mandatory Declarations:** Name, Ingredients, Additives, Net Qty, Mfg Date, Expiry, Batch No., Mfg Address, FSSAI Logo + License, Country of Origin, Veg/Non-Veg Symbol, Nutritional Info, Allergens, Storage, Customer Care, Instructions, Statutory Warnings, MRP. ✅ **Veg Symbol:** 🟢 Green dot inside Green square, near brand name. ✅ **Non-Veg Symbol:** 🔴 Brown dot (NOT red) inside Brown square, near brand name. ✅ **Date Format:** **DD/MM/YY** (or DD/MM/YYYY). ✅ **Font Size:** Min **3 mm** for name/net qty/FSSAI logo; **2 mm** for other declarations. ✅ **Atwater Factors:** P = 4, C = 4, F = 9 kcal/g — **memorize this**. ✅ **Ingredients Order:** Descending by weight (highest first). ✅ **% RDA:** Calculated **per serving**, based on 2000 kcal reference diet (adult). ✅ **Nutritional Info:** Declared per 100 g/100 ml AND per serving. ✅ **Allergens:** 8 major categories — gluten, crustacea, eggs, fish, peanuts, soy, milk, tree nuts. ✅ **Infant Foods:** Mandatory warning "Mother's milk is best for the baby." ✅ **Organic Foods:** **Jaivik Bharat Logo** (green with leaf). ✅ **Fortified Foods:** **+F Logo** (blue) for fortified staples. ✅ **Imported Foods:** Country of origin + Importer details mandatory. ✅ **Loose / Unpackaged Foods:** Exempt from detailed labeling, but name & FSSAI Reg. mark needed. ✅ **FSSAI License Format:** 14-digit number, e.g., `10012011002345` (state code first 2 digits). ✅ **Date Marking Rule:** - Shelf life < 3 months → Month & Year (DD/MM/YY optional) - Shelf life > 3 months → Month & Year - Shelf life > 24 months → Only Date of Manufacture required. ✅ **Net Quantity Tolerance (Legal Metrology):** ±1.5% for 100g–5kg packs; ±1% for > 5kg. ✅ **Key Difference:** "Best Before" = quality deterioration; "Use By / Expiry" = safety threshold. ✅ **Statutory Warnings:** Alcohol — "Consumption of alcohol is injurious to health"; Cigarettes — As per COTPA, 2003. ✅ **Most Examined Topics:** Nutritional calculations, Veg/Non-veg symbol, list of mandatory declarations, date format rules. ✅ **Codex Alimentarius CXS 1-1985:** International standard; India's FSSAI is largely aligned with this. ✅ **Engineering Application:** Used in QA/QC labs, packaging line automation, regulatory affairs, product development. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — Mandatory Labeling Provision** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PEAGT633 | B.Tech Food Technology*

topics.title: Food Adulteration - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Food Adulteration - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Food Adulteration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (FSSAI - Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006)**
> *"An article of food shall be deemed to be adulterated if the article offered by the manufacturer, distributor or dealer for sale is —*
> *(a) not of the nature, substance or quality demanded by the purchaser; or*
> *(b) which contains any extraneous substance; or*
> *(c) which contains any poisonous or other deleterious substance; or*
> *(d) which consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, rotten, decomposed or diseased animal or vegetable substance; or*
> *(e) which is unfit for human consumption."*

In simpler academic terms used in KTU 2024 Scheme PEAGT633:

$$\text{Food Adulteration} = \text{Addition of inferior/missing} \rightarrow \text{Substance} + \text{Withdrawal of essential constituents}$$

### 1.2 Mathematical / Logical Representation

$$\text{Adulterated Food} = (F_{original} - F_{removed}) + F_{foreign} \quad \text{where}$$

- $F_{original}$ = Original genuine food material
- $F_{removed}$ = Useful/essential constituent taken away
- $F_{foreign}$ = Inferior/harmful extraneous substance added

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Adulteration is a deliberate act (or sometimes accidental negligence) that compromises the **quality**, **safety**, **purity**, or **nutritive value** of a food product.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a student copying an exam answer:**
> Imagine you order a glass of **pure apple juice** in a juice shop. You pay ₹200 expecting 100% apple. Instead, the shopkeeper adds water, sugar syrup, and a bit of apple essence to stretch the juice and earn more profit. **The look and color might match, the taste might even feel close, but the genuine content has been reduced and foreign substances have been added.**
>
> **This is exactly what food adulteration is** — deceiving the consumer about the **nature, substance, or quality** of food for economic gain.

### 1.4 Three Pillars of Adulteration

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Nature** | Different from what is claimed | Selling mustard oil as groundnut oil |
| **Substance** | Contains harmful/foreign matter | Lead chromate in turmeric |
| **Quality** | Inferior to demanded standard | Diluted milk, starch in ghee |

---

## 1.5 Types of Adulterants (KTU Syllabus Standard Classification)

```text
┌──────────────────────────┐
│   FOOD ADULTERATION      │
└────────────┬─────────────┘
             │
   ┌─────────┼─────────────────────┐
   │         │                     │
   ▼         ▼                     ▼
INTENTIONAL  ACCIDENTAL          INCIDENTAL
(Deliberate) (Unintentional)    (Environment)

Examples:    Examples:           Examples:
• Chalk in   • Pesticide        • Lead from
  milk         residue in          contaminated
• Vanaspati    fruits              soil in
  in ghee    • Rodent hair       vegetables
• Soap in     in stored grain   • Microbial
  milk                           toxins in
                                  stored food
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Definition (WHO Perspective):**
> The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies adulteration as a public health hazard when the added substance renders the food **injurious to health**, or when a valuable constituent is **wholly or in part abstracted**, or when the food is so processed that its nature, quality, or substance is affected.

### 1.6 Standard Metric Used in Food Safety

$$\text{Purity Index (PI)} = \frac{\text{Mass of genuine component}}{\text{Total mass of sample}} \times 100$$

- A **PI of 100%** indicates completely pure food.
- Any deviation below 100% indicates the presence of adulterants.
- Statutory minimums are prescribed under **FSSAI Standards (Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011)**.
]<]minimax[>[</thinking>

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT633 - FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Food Adulteration - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Food Adulteration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (FSSAI - Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, Section 3)**
> *"An article of food shall be deemed to be adulterated if the article offered by sale is —*
> *(a) not of the nature, substance or quality demanded by the purchaser; or*
> *(b) which contains any extraneous substance; or*
> *(c) which contains any poisonous or other deleterious substance; or*
> *(d) which consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, rotten, decomposed or diseased animal or vegetable substance; or*
> *(e) which is unfit for human consumption."*

In simpler academic terms used in KTU 2024 Scheme PEAGT633:

$$\text{Food Adulteration} = \text{Substitution/Inferior Addition} + \text{Withdrawal of Essential Constituents}$$

### 1.2 Mathematical / Logical Representation

$$\text{Adulterated Food} = (F_{original} - F_{removed}) + F_{foreign}$$

- $F_{original}$ = Original genuine food material
- $F_{removed}$ = Useful/essential constituent abstracted
- $F_{foreign}$ = Inferior/harmful extraneous substance added

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Adulteration compromises the **quality**, **safety**, **purity**, or **nutritive value** of a food product.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like ordering a pure apple juice:**
> You pay ₹200 expecting 100% apple juice. Instead, the vendor adds water, sugar syrup, and a dash of apple essence to stretch the quantity and earn more profit. The **look, color, and taste** might seem close, but the genuine nutritional content has been reduced and foreign substances have been added.
>
> This is exactly what **food adulteration** is — deceiving the consumer about the **nature, substance, or quality** of food, often for economic gain.

### 1.4 Three Pillars of Adulteration (FSSAI Framework)

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Nature** | Different from what is claimed | Selling mustard oil as groundnut oil |
| **Substance** | Contains harmful/foreign matter | Lead chromate in turmeric |
| **Quality** | Inferior to demanded standard | Diluted milk, starch in ghee |

---

## 1.5 Types of Adulteration (KTU Syllabus Standard Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification based on Intent:**

1. **Intentional Adulteration** — Deliberate, for profit. E.g., chalk in milk, vanaspati in ghee.
2. **Accidental Adulteration** — Unintentional negligence. E.g., pesticide residue, rodent hair in grain.
3. **Incidental Adulteration** — Environmental/industrial contamination. E.g., lead in soil-grown vegetables, microbial toxins.

### 1.6 Standard Metric Used in Food Safety

$$\text{Purity Index (PI)} = \frac{\text{Mass of genuine component}}{\text{Total mass of sample}} \times 100$$

- A **PI of 100%** indicates completely pure food.
- Any deviation below 100% indicates the presence of adulterants.
- Statutory minimums are prescribed under **FSSAI Standards (Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The "What, Why, How" of Adulteration

### **WHAT is Food Adulteration?**

Adulteration is the act of **deliberately debasing** the quality of food offered for sale either by:

- **Admixture** (mixing inferior substances)
- **Substitution** (wholly replacing genuine food with look-alikes)
- **Abstraction** (removing valuable constituents like fat, vitamins)
- **Concealment** (hiding defective quality through processing)

### **WHY does Adulteration Happen?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The primary driver is **economic motive** — increasing volume or apparent quality without proportionate cost.

| Driver | Mechanism |
|--------|-----------|
| **Profit Maximization** | Bulking agents (water, starch) increase weight/volume at low cost |
| **Aesthetic Enhancement** | Artificial colors make spoiled food appear fresh |
| **Shelf Life Manipulation** | Chemical preservatives hide poor storage conditions |
| **Camouflage of Spoilage** | Additives mask foul odor/taste of decomposed food |
| **Tax/Cost Evasion** | Substituting expensive ingredients with cheap alternatives |

### **HOW is Adulteration Executed?**

$$\text{Adulteration Pathway} = \underbrace{S}_{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{selection}} \underbrace{A}_{Adulterant} \xrightarrow{\text{addition}} \underbrace{F}_{Food} \xrightarrow{\text{sale}} \underbrace{C}_{Consumer}$$

- **S (Source):** Cheap, often non-food-grade material.
- **A (Adulterant):** Must mimic genuine food in appearance, texture, or color.
- **F (Food):** Targeted vehicle (milk, ghee, spices, oils).
- **C (Consumer):** Unaware victim, often economically vulnerable.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Unit / Limit | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | **Purity Index** | $PI = \dfrac{m_{genuine}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$ | Percentage (\%) | $<100\%$ = adulterated |
| 2 | **Adulteration Mass Balance** | $m_{sample} = m_{genuine} + m_{adulterant}$ | grams (g) | Conservation of mass |
| 3 | **Adulterant Fraction** | $x_{add} = \dfrac{m_{adulterant}}{m_{sample}}$ | Dimensionless | Detection threshold often $\leq 0.1\%$ |
| 4 | **Economic Gain Ratio** | $G = \dfrac{C_{sold} - C_{actual}}{C_{actual}}$ | Ratio | Driver of intentional adulteration |
| 5 | **Toxicological Risk** | $R = \dfrac{Dose_{exposure} \times Frequency}{ADI}$ | Dimensionless | $ADI$ = Acceptable Daily Intake |
| 6 | **FSSAI Statutory Limit** | $\sum \text{Adulterant} \leq \text{Permissible Limit}$ | mg/kg or ppm | Listed per commodity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When calculating purity, **NEVER** confuse adulterant mass with water content for commodities like milk. Use standardized formulas like the **Gerber method** (for fat) and **lactometer reading** (corrected for temperature) for milk purity analysis.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industry Relevance

Food Adulteration detection is a **multi-disciplinary engineering problem**:

- **Chemical Engineering:** Designing chromatographic (HPLC, GC) detection systems.
- **Biotechnology:** DNA-based detection of species substitution (e.g., pig fat in ghee).
- **Instrumentation:** FTIR, NIR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry for adulterant identification.
- **Data Science:** Machine learning models trained on spectral signatures of adulterated food.
- **Public Health Policy:** FSSAI enforcement, BIS standards, AGMARK certification.

$$\text{Signal}_{\text{detection}} = f(\lambda, T, P, c_{analyte}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Purity Quantification}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Career Insight for KTU Students:** Food safety is a growing sector in Kerala's spice, coconut, and marine export industries. Engineers with expertise in analytical instrumentation are in high demand at FSSAI-recognised labs (NABL-accredited) and in HACCP-certified food processing units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Purity Index from Mass Balance

### **Given:**
A sample of turmeric powder has total mass $m_{sample} = 105\text{ g}$, and laboratory analysis reveals that it contains $100\text{ g}$ of genuine turmeric and $5\text{ g}$ of lead chromate (PbCrO₄) as an artificial colorant.

### **Step 1 — Establish the Mass Balance Equation**

By conservation of mass in the sample:

$$m_{sample} = m_{genuine} + m_{adulterant}$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$105\text{ g} = 100\text{ g} + m_{adulterant}$$

### **Step 3 — Solve for Adulterant Mass**

$$m_{adulterant} = 105 - 100 = 5\text{ g}$$

### **Step 4 — Apply the Purity Index Formula**

$$PI = \frac{m_{genuine}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$$

### **Step 5 — Substitute and Compute**

$$PI = \frac{100}{105} \times 100$$

### **Step 6 — Final Result**

$$PI = 95.24\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the purity index is high (95.24%), the adulterant here is **lead chromate — a known carcinogen**. Therefore, even minute quantities of **toxic adulterants** can render food unsafe, regardless of the apparent purity index. This is why FSSAI enforces **zero-tolerance limits** for certain poisons.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Adulterant Fraction Calculation

### **Given:**
A milk sample is tested using a lactometer. The lactometer reading indicates that $9\text{ g}$ of added urea is present in a $1000\text{ mL}$ milk sample. Density of milk $\rho_{milk} = 1.030 \text{ g/mL}$.

### **Step 1 — Calculate Total Sample Mass**

$$m_{sample} = V \times \rho_{milk}$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$m_{sample} = 1000 \text{ mL} \times 1.030 \text{ g/mL}$$

### **Step 3 — Compute**

$$m_{sample} = 1030\text{ g}$$

### **Step 4 — Apply the Adulterant Fraction Formula**

$$x_{add} = \frac{m_{adulterant}}{m_{sample}}$$

### **Step 5 — Substitute and Solve**

$$x_{add} = \frac{9}{1030}$$

### **Step 6 — Express as Percentage**

$$x_{add} \times 100 = \frac{9}{1030} \times 100$$

### **Step 7 — Final Result**

$$x_{add} = 0.874\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If $x_{add}$ exceeds the FSSAI permissible limit (for urea in milk, generally undetectable to trace levels only), the food is **legally adulterated** under Section 3 of the FSSAI Act, 2006.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Adulterants vs. Test Methods

| Food Item | Common Adulterant | Health Hazard | Standard Detection Test |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **Milk** | Water, urea, starch, detergent | Kidney damage, gastric issues | Lactometer, Gerber fat test |
| **Ghee** | Vanaspati, animal fat, starch | Cardiac risk, adulteration | Baudouin test, Reichert value |
| **Turmeric** | Lead chromate, metanil yellow | Cancer, brain damage | HCl test, colour reaction |
| **Chilli powder** | Brick dust, artificial dye | Liver/kidney toxicity | Water sedimentation test |
| **Honey** | Sugar syrup, commercial glucose | Diabetes, no nutrition | Fiehe's test, specific gravity |
| **Tea leaves** | Exhausted tea, colourants | Liver toxicity | Chlorophyll extraction test |
| **Mustard oil** | Argemone oil | Epidemic dropsy | Nitric acid test |
| **Coconut oil** | Mineral oil | Cancer, GI irritation | KOH saponification test |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Detecting Starch in Milk

### **Problem Statement:**
$50\text{ mL}$ of a milk sample is boiled, cooled, and tested with iodine solution. A blue-black colour develops. Quantitative analysis shows $0.25\text{ g}$ of starch present. Density of milk = $1.028\text{ g/mL}$. Determine purity index.

### **Step 1 — Determine Sample Mass**

$$m_{sample} = 50 \times 1.028 = 51.4\text{ g}$$

### **Step 2 — Determine Genuine Milk Mass**

$$m_{genuine} = 51.4 - 0.25 = 51.15\text{ g}$$

### **Step 3 — Apply Purity Formula**

$$PI = \frac{51.15}{51.4} \times 100$$

### **Step 4 — Final Answer**

$$PI = 99.51\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Insight:** Even a 0.49% deviation in PI is **legally significant** for milk because starch is a **non-permitted additive**. The legal test is not the PI value alone, but whether **any non-declared adulterant is present** beyond the limit of detection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Food Adulteration Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Material] --> B{Market Demand}
    B --> C[Economic Incentive]
    C --> D[Selection of Adulterant]
    D --> E[Mixing or Substitution]
    E --> F[Adulterated Food Product]
    F --> G[Distribution and Sale]
    G --> H[Consumer Purchase]
    H --> I{Detection Method}
    I -->|Detected| J[Recall and Legal Action]
    I -->|Undetected| K[Health Impact on Consumer]
    K --> L[Disease or Nutritional Deficit]
    L --> M[Public Health Burden]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Adulteration Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Food Adulteration] --> B[Intentional]
    A --> C[Accidental]
    A --> D[Incidental]
    B --> B1[For Profit]
    B --> B2[For Aesthetic Camouflage]
    C --> C1[Pesticide Residue]
    C --> C2[Packaging Contamination]
    D --> D1[Environmental Pollutants]
    D --> D2[Microbial Toxins]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sample Collection] --> S2[Sample Preparation]
    S2 --> S3[Physical Tests]
    S2 --> S4[Chemical Tests]
    S2 --> S5[Instrumental Tests]
    S3 --> R1[Visual and Sensory]
    S4 --> R2[Reagent Based Reaction]
    S5 --> R3[Spectroscopy and Chromatography]
    R1 --> O[Result Interpretation]
    R2 --> O
    R3 --> O
    O --> P{Compliant with FSSAI}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Marked Safe]
    P -->|No| T[Declared Adulterated]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Adulteration Detection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Suspected Food Sample] --> Q1{Is the substance what is claimed?}
    Q1 -->|No| AD1[NATURE Adulteration]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does it contain harmful foreign matter?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| AD2[SUBSTANCE Adulteration]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the quality below standard?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| AD3[QUALITY Adulteration]
    Q3 -->|No| SAFE[Not Adulterated]
    AD1 --> LEGAL[Legal Action under FSSAI Section 3]
    AD2 --> LEGAL
    AD3 --> LEGAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Define food adulteration. List any four common adulterants used in milk.**

**Model Answer:**

> Food adulteration, as defined under Section 3 of the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, is the act of rendering a food article inferior in nature, substance, or quality by admixture, substitution, abstraction, or concealment of harmful substances.
>
> **Four Common Adulterants in Milk:**
>
> 1. **Water** — added to increase volume.
> 2. **Urea** — added to artificially raise the apparent protein content (nitrogen reading).
> 3. **Starch** — added to increase viscosity and thickness.
> 4. **Detergent** — added to produce artificial froth, mimicking the natural fat-rich foam of pure milk.
>
> [3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing adulterants]

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Differentiate between intentional and incidental adulteration with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Intentional Adulteration | Incidental Adulteration |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Definition** | Deliberate addition of inferior/harmful substances | Unintentional contamination from environment or processing |
| **Motive** | Economic gain | Lack of proper hygiene or industrial pollution |
| **Actor** | Trader, manufacturer, or distributor | Can occur at any stage — farm, storage, transport |
| **Example 1** | Adding chalk powder to milk | Lead from industrial soil contaminating leafy vegetables |
| **Example 2** | Mixing vanaspati ghee in pure ghee | Microbial toxins developing in improperly stored grain |
| **Legal Severity** | Prosecution under FSSAI, imprisonment up to 6 months–life | Improvement notices, corrective action, possible prosecution |

> [3 Marks: Comparison expected with examples. Award 1.5 marks per side.]

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Past Pattern]`

#### **(a)** Define food adulteration as per the FSSAI Act, 2006. Explain the three pillars (nature, substance, quality) on which the definition is built. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Under Section 3 of the FSSAI Act, 2006, food is deemed adulterated if it does not conform to the nature, substance, or quality demanded by the purchaser, contains harmful or extraneous matter, or is unfit for human consumption.

**Three Pillars:**

1. **Nature Pillar:** The food must be of the **declared type**. Selling groundnut oil labelled as mustard oil violates the nature pillar.

2. **Substance Pillar:** The food must be free of **extraneous, poisonous, or deleterious matter**. Lead chromate in turmeric or argemone oil in mustard oil violates this pillar.

3. **Quality Pillar:** The food must meet the **declared standard of quality**. Diluted milk, watered-down honey, or below-grade ghee violates this pillar.

> **Conclusion:** Any breach of any of the three pillars constitutes legal adulteration under the FSSAI Act.

> [Award 2 marks for definition, 4 marks for pillar explanation with 1 example each, 1 mark for conclusion]

#### **(b)** A sample of turmeric powder weighs 110 g. Laboratory analysis shows that 8 g of metanil yellow (an azo dye) is present. Calculate the purity index and comment on its safety. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass Balance:**

$$m_{sample} = m_{genuine} + m_{adulterant}$$
$$110 = m_{genuine} + 8$$
$$m_{genuine} = 102\text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Purity Index:**

$$PI = \frac{102}{110} \times 100 = 92.73\%$$

**Step 3 — Safety Comment:**

Although the purity index is relatively high (92.73%), the adulterant **metanil yellow** is a **banned food colour** under FSSAI regulations. It is classified as a **probable carcinogen** and is associated with liver damage, tumours, and developmental toxicity.

**Step 4 — Legal Status:**

$$\text{Since metanil yellow is a non-permitted substance, PI} = 0\% \text{ from a legal standpoint.}$$

> **Conclusion:** The food is legally adulterated and unsafe, regardless of the calculated PI value, because the adulterant is a banned toxic substance.

> [Award 2 marks for mass balance, 2 marks for PI calculation, 2 marks for safety comment, 1 mark for legal conclusion]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Past Pattern]`

#### **(a)** Classify food adulterants based on intent. Discuss intentional adulteration in detail with five real-world examples. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification based on intent:**

1. **Intentional Adulteration** — Deliberate, profit-driven.
2. **Accidental Adulteration** — Unintentional negligence.
3. **Incidental Adulteration** — Environmental/industrial contamination.

**Intentional Adulteration — Detailed Examples:**

| # | Food | Adulterant | Purpose |
|---|------|------------|---------|
| 1 | Milk | Water, urea, starch | Increase volume and apparent protein content |
| 2 | Ghee | Vanaspati, animal fat | Cost reduction |
| 3 | Turmeric | Lead chromate | Bright yellow colour enhancement |
| 4 | Chilli powder | Brick dust, artificial dye | Increased bulk |
| 5 | Honey | Commercial sugar syrup | Cost reduction |

**Common Intentional Adulterants:**

- **Foreign matter:** Sand, chalk, stones, brick dust.
- **Spoiled food:** Repackaging decomposed items.
- **Inferior substitutes:** Vanaspati in ghee, argemone in mustard oil.
- **Artificial enhancers:** Toxic dyes (metanil yellow, lead chromate).

> [Award 2 marks for classification, 4 marks for examples in tabular form, 1 mark for common adulterants list]

#### **(b)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the step-by-step detection pathway for suspected adulterated food in a quality control laboratory. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Detection Pathway Flowchart:**

```
Sample Collection → Sample Preparation → Physical Tests → Chemical Tests → Instrumental Tests → Result Interpretation → Compliance Decision
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Sample Collection:** Random/coded sampling from market as per FSSAI sampling protocol (Food Safety and Standards (Sampling and Analysis) Regulations, 2011).

2. **Sample Preparation:** Homogenisation, drying, extraction, or digestion depending on matrix.

3. **Physical Tests:**
   - Visual inspection (colour, texture, foreign matter).
   - Sensory evaluation (odour, taste).
   - Specific gravity, refractive index, moisture content.

4. **Chemical Tests:**
   - **Baudouin test** for vanaspati in ghee.
   - **Fiehe's test** for sugar syrup in honey.
   - **Nitric acid test** for argemone oil in mustard oil.
   - **Iodine test** for starch in milk.

5. **Instrumental Tests:**
   - HPLC, GC-MS for pesticide residues.
   - FTIR for oil adulteration.
   - PCR for species identification in meat products.

6. **Result Interpretation:** Compare observed values with **FSSAI Codex/FSSAI standards**.

7. **Compliance Decision:**
   - **Compliant:** Safe for consumption.
   - **Non-Compliant:** Adulterated — legal action under FSSAI Section 3, recall, prosecution.

> [Award 3 marks for flowchart, 3 marks for step explanation, 1 mark for legal implication]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Misinterpreting Purity Index:** Students often report a high PI (e.g., 95%) and conclude the food is "safe." This is **WRONG**. If the adulterant is a **banned toxic substance** (lead, mercury, metanil yellow), even trace amounts make the food **legally adulterated**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Intentional with Incidental:** Intentional is profit-driven and deliberate; Incidental is environmental. Do not interchange them.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Legal Reference:** Always cite **FSSAI Act 2006, Section 3** when defining adulteration in long-answer questions. Marks are awarded specifically for legal terminology.
>
> 4. **Calculation Errors:** Remember to convert mL to g using density before applying purity formulas. Density of milk ≈ 1.028–1.032 g/mL.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Units:** Purity Index is in %; adulterant fraction can be in ppm, %, or dimensionless depending on context.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must Remember for KTU 2024 PEAGT633 Module 3:**

- ⭐ **Definition Source:** Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, **Section 3**.
- ⭐ **Three Pillars:** **Nature**, **Substance**, **Quality**.
- ⭐ **Classification:** Intentional, Accidental, Incidental.
- ⭐ **Key Formula:** $PI = \dfrac{m_{genuine}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$.
- ⭐ **Adulterant Fraction:** $x_{add} = \dfrac{m_{adulterant}}{m_{sample}}$.
- ⭐ **Common Adulterants:** Water (milk), chalk (milk), vanaspati (ghee), lead chromate (turmeric), metanil yellow (dal), argemone oil (mustard oil), brick dust (chilli powder), sugar syrup (honey).
- ⭐ **Common Tests:** Lactometer (milk), Baudouin (ghee), Fiehe's (honey), Nitric acid (mustard oil), Iodine (starch), HCl (turmeric).
- ⭐ **Regulatory Body:** **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India).
- ⭐ **Punishment:** Imprisonment from 6 months to life, plus fines, depending on severity (Section 59 of FSSAI Act, 2006).
- ⭐ **Golden Rule:** If adulterant is **banned or toxic**, PI is **irrelevant** — food is **legally adulterated**.
